project.title: Blok Core Redesign
project.body: Replace Blok's $/js/ctx workflow-authoring surface with a typed, handle-based DSL (workflow(entry => { const x = step(...); ... }), structural {$ref} references, tpl templates, op-based branch/forEach) that compiles to a language-neutral JSON IR — while keeping the runner engine (PersistenceHelper + Mapper), defineNode, and all 7 sidecar runtimes byte-identical, consolidating runner+shared+helper into one tree-shakeable @blokjs/core, ending lockstep release, killing the hand-written Nodes.ts via import-registration + generated runtime stubs, and round-tripping the IR losslessly through a Studio canvas and the marketplace registry. The founder's bar is 'nothing lost': every existing workflow (1611 TS + 545 JSON), every trigger, every control-flow primitive, and every runtime must produce identical resolved-IR and run traces after the change, with every soundness gap converted into a compile error or a loud runtime failure rather than a silent miscompile.

milestones.title: M1 — Foundation — freeze the contracts
milestones.description: Resolve the three contract-level forks that every later epic inherits, and prove the engine is genuinely byte-identical against the real corpus. Nothing downstream is safe until these are decided: (1) the IR input-value wire format — structural {$ref} object vs lowered js/ctx.state string — probed against the REAL mapper (which only string-resolves and recurses into plain containers, so a {$ref} object is walked into and never resolved); (2) the canonical node-key rule (node.name != registry key today: api-call is named 'api-call' but keyed '@blokjs/api-call'); (3) the handle TYPE-SYSTEM soundness rules (ephemeral handles unreadable, as/spread relocate the root, per-item handles closure-scoped, duplicate-id hard-throw). Ship the full-corpus equivalence gate here as the standing 'nothing lost' proof. No authoring sugar, no canvas, no codegen until M1 is green.

milestones.title: M2 — Authoring surface, packaging & migration
milestones.description: With the wire format, key rule, and handle soundness fixed, build the deliverables that consume them: the @blokjs/core package (exports-map subpath split + sideEffects:false so DSL-only import does not pull grpc/otel/sqlite; deprecated re-export shims over the 168/145/114-file blast radius; float core off lockstep while triggers/nodes/cli stay co-versioned); the runtimeNode stub codegen + blokctl nodes sync over the already-built ListNodes infra (with no-schema degradation to unknown, not silent any); the typed entry handles for all 10 triggers incl. greenfield manual; tpl; and the field-aware codemods with an honest coverage report marking the un-migratable majority. Migration ships behind a schemaVersion gate, no-op-by-default, with @blokjs/expr and non-HTTP workflows proven byte-identical.

milestones.title: M3 — Canvas round-trip + JSON twin
milestones.description: Make the IR editable and round-trippable in Studio: stable step-id-derived node ids (replace the build-order idCounter that renumbers synthetic nodes on every structural edit), a layout decision (separate layout.json vs inline ui:{x,y}, reconciled with the synthetic-node-has-no-step-id problem), lossless edit ops over branch/switch/forEach/tryCatch nesting, rename propagation across EVERY documented reference site (incl. raw-ctx branch.when and tpl), a connect-picker that emits persistence-shape-correct refs (spread/as/ephemeral aware), and a hard duplicate-id reject before save. Round-trip is JSON-only; TS workflows stay read-only but may carry layout.

milestones.title: M4 — Bulletproof — e2e, perf, marketplace seam, docs
milestones.description: Close the platform-level seams and make the guarantees standing rather than one-shot: validate the IR as the registry/marketplace publish+install unit (validateWorkflow as a real admission gate, install-time stub resolution); proto-conformance CI across all 7 SDKs upstream of stub codegen; rewrite the canonical authoring docs (context-and-state.mdx + AGENTS.md + CLAUDE.md) to teach the handle model and remove $/js/ guidance while keeping the node-side ctx ABI; and wire the full-corpus equivalence gate + nodes sync --check + bundle-size assertion into CI so 'nothing lost' and 'DSL stays light' cannot silently regress.

epics.title: Adversarial validation
epics.body:
The IR-as-formalized-v2-Zod approach is sound and high-leverage (mostly deletion of 2 stale hand-written schemas + 1 generated artifact). But I broke three load-bearing claims. (1) BYTE-IDENTICAL ENGINE IS FALSE for the redesign's `{ $ref: { step, path } }` input value: the real mapper (core/shared/src/utils/Mapper.ts:269-288) only resolves STRING values and recurses INTO plain object containers — it would walk into `{ $ref }` and try to string-resolve `step`/`path`, never resolving it as a reference. Either the DSL must compile `{ $ref }` back to a `js/ctx.state...` STRING before the mapper sees it (then it's not really a structured-ref IR, it's the same js-strings), or the mapper needs a NEW `$ref` branch (an engine change). The spec/deck claim "same Mapper resolves the same { $ref }, identical runtime" is not true of today's code. (2) THE JSON LOAD PATH NEVER VALIDATES AGAINST THE ZOD SCHEMA: triggers/http/src/runner/scanWorkflows.ts does JSON.parse → normalizeWorkflow (structural, tolerant reads), zero Zod. So "one published schema is the single validation source everywhere" is aspirational — and wiring validateWorkflow() as a mandatory JSON gate is a BEHAVIOR CHANGE that can reject workflows that load fine today, because the 18 `.strict()` step schemas are stricter than the normalizer's structural read. (3) `.strict()` EVERYWHERE: all 8 step kinds + nested arms = 18 `.strict()` calls in StepOpts.ts. The TS `workflow()` factory runs `V2StepSchema.safeParse` per step, so adding `ui`/`schemaVersion` requires editing every strict schema (incl. nested then/else/forEach-body/switch-case/trycatch schemas) AND the WorkflowOpts TS interface AND every InternalStep constructor (8 of them, enumerated-field copies — the index signature does NOT auto-copy). Miss one and `ui` either throws (strict) or silently vanishes (normalizer). Also: zod-to-json-schema is a devDependency, not a dependency (spec says dependency); shipping validateWorkflow() from @blokjs/helper for the registry/blokctl is fine, but the schema export needs files:["dist","schemas"].

**Verdict:** `sound-with-risks`

**Risks / hard cases found in validation:**
- BYTE-IDENTICAL ENGINE CLAIM IS FALSE for structured `{ $ref }` input values. core/shared/src/utils/Mapper.ts:269-288 (replaceObjectStrings) only resolves `typeof value === 'string'` and recurses into plain containers (isPlainContainer, :235). A `{ $ref: { step, path } }` object is a plain container → mapper recurses INTO it, tries to string-resolve `step` and `path`, and NEVER resolves the ref. Resolution: the DSL compiles `{ $ref }` to a `js/ctx.state.<step>.<path>` STRING before serialization (so the runtime IR carries strings, not the structured object — consistent with 'the string still exists in the IR' but contradicts 'the mapper resolves the same { $ref }'), OR add a real `$ref` branch to the mapper (engine change, not byte-identical). This must be a SPIKE before the IR shape is frozen, because it decides whether the published IR's input values are strings or objects — a contract-level decision.
- JSON workflows are NEVER Zod-validated today: scanWorkflows.ts does JSON.parse → normalizeWorkflow (tolerant structural reads, WorkflowNormalizer.ts:162). The 18 `.strict()` step schemas are STRICTER than the normalizer. Wiring validateWorkflow() as a mandatory JSON admission gate WILL reject some workflows that currently load and run — any JSON file carrying an extra field the normalizer ignores but `.strict()` rejects. This is a silent behavior change disguised as 'add a validator'. Must be opt-in / warn-first, with a corpus test over all existing JSON workflows.
- 18 `.strict()` calls in StepOpts.ts across 8 step kinds + nested arms (branch then/else :373/:380, forEach body :709/:715, switch cases :811/:817, tryCatch :884/:890). `ui` must be added to ALL of them or it throws in the TS factory (workflowV2.ts:229 runs V2StepSchema.safeParse per step). Miss a nested-arm schema and `ui` on a step inside a branch/forEach throws while `ui` on a top-level step works — confusing, position-dependent failure.
- InternalStep constructors copy ENUMERATED fields only (8 constructors: regular :382, branch :516, subworkflow :572, wait :697, forEach :760, loop :827, switch ~860, tryCatch :953). The `[key:string]: unknown` index signature (WorkflowNormalizer.ts:98) is a TYPE affordance, not a runtime copy — verified normalizeRegularStep hand-lists every field. `ui` is dropped on the floor unless added to all 8 + the recursive inner-step builders. So `ui` survives the TS schema but vanishes at normalize → toJson() round-trip loses it → canvas round-trip breaks. This is the exact failure the 'ui pass-through' goal exists to prevent.
- The TS author surface (WorkflowOpts interface, WorkflowOpts.ts:39-98 and the V2Step type) has no `schemaVersion`/`ui` fields. Adding them to the Zod schema alone is insufficient — the hand-written TS interfaces that authors program against need the fields too, or TS authors can't set them with type safety (only the Zod runtime accepts them).
- zod-to-json-schema is a devDependency (^3.25.2), not a runtime dependency — the spec (§S1 and its 'verified' note) calls it a dependency. The generator (build-schema.ts) runs at build time so devDep is correct, BUT if validateWorkflow() at runtime needs to emit/compare schemas it'd need the runtime dep. validateWorkflow() as written (safeParse only) does NOT need it, so this is fine — but the spec's factual claim is wrong and any plan that runtime-generates the schema breaks.
- Studio has no @blokjs/helper dependency (spec verified this). Adding it ships Zod + the full StepOpts schema graph into the Studio browser bundle. The recursive z.lazy schemas are non-trivial; bundle-size impact is real and unmeasured. Spec offers fallback (b) server-side endpoint but defers the measurement.
- schemaVersion as `z.literal('2').default('2')` on a NON-strict envelope is fine, but if anyone later makes WorkflowV2Schema `.strict()` (to match step strictness), every workflow carrying input/output/events/middleware still validates — but a v1 JSON file fed to validateWorkflow() would explode unhelpfully. The v1-vs-v2 discrimination is structural in the normalizer, NOT in the schema; validateWorkflow() will reject all v1 files with cryptic strict errors. Open question 4 in the spec hand-waves this.

**Open questions to resolve:**
- Does the runtime IR carry `{ $ref: { step, path } }` as a structured object, or does the DSL lower it to a `js/ctx.state...` string before serialization? This is THE contract decision and it determines whether the mapper needs changing. The deck says both things in different places. Resolve before freezing the published schema — the JSON Schema's `inputs` value type depends on it.
- Is validateWorkflow() advisory (warn, Studio rendering hint, registry gate) or an enforced admission gate on load? If enforced on the JSON load path it's a breaking change. Recommend: advisory + registry-publish-only enforcement; never block scanWorkflows load in v2.
- Does the published schema describe ONLY v2, treating v1 as legacy/normalizer-only (spec recommends this)? If so, validateWorkflow() must structurally detect v1 and skip/route it, not strict-reject it — otherwise every v1 file fails validation with strict-mode noise.
- Should WorkflowV2Schema itself become `.strict()` for parity with steps, or stay open so input/output/events/middleware/schemaVersion/ui all pass? Currently open. If it stays open, `schemaVersion` typos (`schemaVesion`) pass silently. If it goes strict, it must enumerate every metadata field.
- Can the Studio bundle absorb Zod + the full StepOpts schema graph, or is the server-side validate endpoint (§7.5b) actually required for M5? Needs a measured bundle-size delta, not a recommendation.
- Where does the published `$id` resolve? If it's just an identifier (spec's recommendation) AI constrained-decoding is fine, but VS Code's json.schemas needs a real file path — confirm the extension bundles a generated copy and the CI equality test guards it.

tasks.title: SPIKE: decide IR input-value representation — `{ $ref }` object vs lowered js-string — by probing the real mapper
tasks.body:
The load-bearing claim is 'the same Mapper resolves the same `{ $ref }`, byte-identical runtime'. It is false as stated: core/shared/src/utils/Mapper.ts:269-288 (replaceObjectStrings) resolves only `typeof value === 'string'` and recurses into plain object containers via isPlainContainer (:235). A `{ $ref: { step, path } }` value is a plain container → the mapper recurses INTO it and string-resolves its `step`/`path` fields, never treating it as a reference. Decide, with a runnable probe, between two options: (A) DSL lowers every handle read to a `js/ctx.state.<step>.<path>` STRING at compile/serialize time so the runtime IR carries strings (mapper unchanged — truly byte-identical, and the published schema's `inputs` value type is `string | scalar | nested`); or (B) add a new `$ref` resolution branch to replaceObjectStrings that detects `{ $ref }` and resolves against state[step] (engine change — NOT byte-identical, but keeps structured refs in the runtime IR). Write a throwaway test that feeds both shapes through the real Mapper against a populated ctx and shows what each produces. Output: a one-paragraph decision + the failing/passing probe.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] A runnable probe demonstrates the current mapper's behavior on a `{ $ref: { step, path } }` input value (it does NOT resolve it as a ref)
- [ ] A written decision: option A (lower to js-string, mapper untouched) or option B (new mapper branch), with the runtime/contract tradeoff stated
- [ ] If option B: the exact diff site in Mapper.ts:283 identified and the byte-identical claim formally retracted in the IR contract doc

**Edge cases to test:**
- `{ $ref }` nested inside an array input value
- `{ $ref }` whose `path` is empty (whole-step reference)
- an input object that legitimately has a user key named `$ref` (collision — grep confirmed no current usage, but the IR reserves it)
- a forEach per-item handle ref resolved inside a sub-workflow step (the cross-workflow recursion guard at Mapper.ts:256-267 must not break)

*estimate M*

tasks.title: Name the IR + add `schemaVersion` to schema AND the TS author interface
tasks.body:
Add `export const WorkflowIRSchema = WorkflowV2Schema; export type WorkflowIR = WorkflowV2; export const WORKFLOW_IR_VERSION = '2'`. Add `schemaVersion: z.literal('2').default('2')` to WorkflowV2Schema (WorkflowOpts.ts:63 — envelope is NON-strict so this is additive). CRITICAL beyond the spec: also add `schemaVersion?: '2'` to the WorkflowOpts TS interface (WorkflowOpts.ts:39-98) so TS authors get the field with type safety, and thread it onto `_config` in workflowV2.ts (the `_config` spread at :276 and toJson at :300 must carry it). Decide whether the envelope stays open (typos pass) or goes strict (must enumerate input/output/events/middleware/ui).

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] WorkflowIRSchema/WorkflowIR/WORKFLOW_IR_VERSION exported from @blokjs/helper index
- [ ] schemaVersion defaults to '2' on parse; absent input still validates
- [ ] TS author can write `workflow({ schemaVersion: '2', ... })` with no type error AND omit it with no type error
- [ ] schemaVersion survives toJson() round-trip

**Edge cases to test:**
- existing JSON file with no schemaVersion → '2'
- JSON file with schemaVersion: '3' (future) → must reject clearly, not silently coerce
- typo `schemaVesion` — confirm whether it passes (open envelope) or fails (strict); document the choice

*estimate S*

tasks.title: TEST: schemaVersion default/round-trip + existing-workflow-corpus regression
tasks.body:
Prove every existing TS and JSON workflow in the repo still validates after schemaVersion is added. Run WorkflowIRSchema.safeParse over the full corpus under triggers/http (both TS-compiled _config and raw JSON). Assert: (1) absent schemaVersion → parsed '2'; (2) explicit '2' → accepted; (3) '3' → rejected; (4) round-trip workflow()→toJson()→JSON.parse preserves schemaVersion; (5) the typo case behaves as T2 decided.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] A corpus test enumerates real workflow files and asserts all parse clean
- [ ] schemaVersion default, accept-2, reject-3, and round-trip cases all asserted
- [ ] Test fails loudly if a real workflow regresses

**Edge cases to test:**
- v1 (steps[]/nodes{}) JSON file in the corpus — must be excluded or routed, NOT strict-rejected
- middleware-only workflow (no trigger) still validates

*estimate S · depends: S1-T2*

tasks.title: Add optional `ui` pass-through to ALL 18 strict step schemas + WorkflowOpts/V2Step TS types
tasks.body:
Add `ui: z.object({ x: z.number().optional(), y: z.number().optional(), notes: z.string().optional() }).passthrough().optional()` to EVERY V2 step schema in StepOpts.ts — all are `.strict()` (18 calls), so a missing one makes `ui` throw on that step kind. Includes the nested-arm inner schemas: branch then/else (:373/:380), forEach body (:709/:715), switch cases (:811/:817), tryCatch try/catch (:884/:890), plus the top-level regular/branch/subworkflow/wait/forEach/loop/switch/tryCatch schemas. Also add `ui?` to the V2Step TS type union so authors set it type-safely. Do NOT touch $refStrategy. ponytail: `.passthrough()` lets the canvas add color/collapsed later without a schema bump — don't enumerate those now.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] A step of EVERY kind (regular, branch, subworkflow, wait, forEach, loop, switch, tryCatch) accepts a `ui` block without throwing
- [ ] A step INSIDE a branch then-arm / forEach body / switch case / tryCatch arm accepts `ui` (nested strict schemas covered)
- [ ] TS authors can set `ui` on any step kind with no type error
- [ ] Omitting `ui` is unaffected

**Edge cases to test:**
- ui on a nested step (branch arm) — the most likely miss
- ui with an extra passthrough key (color) — must pass
- ui: {} empty — must pass
- ui with a wrong-typed x (string) — must reject

*estimate M*

tasks.title: Thread `ui` through all 8 InternalStep constructors + recursive inner-step builders in the normalizer
tasks.body:
The `[key:string]: unknown` index signature on InternalStep (WorkflowNormalizer.ts:98) is a TYPE affordance only — every constructor hand-lists fields (verified: normalizeRegularStep:382 enumerates name/node/type/active/stop/as/spread/ephemeral). Add `...(step.ui ? { ui: step.ui } : {})` to all 8 top-level constructors (regular:382, branch:516, subworkflow:572, wait:697, forEach:760, loop:827, switch:~860, tryCatch:953) AND to the recursive inner-step normalization that builds then/else/forEach-body/switch-case/trycatch inner InternalSteps — otherwise `ui` on a nested step is dropped even though the schema accepted it. The runner ignores `ui` at execution; this is purely so toJson()/canvas round-trip preserves it.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] A workflow with `ui` on top-level AND nested steps round-trips IR → normalize → toJson() with all ui preserved
- [ ] No execution behavior change (ui ignored by the runner)
- [ ] Removing the thread from any one constructor makes the round-trip test for that step kind fail

**Edge cases to test:**
- ui on a step inside a forEach body (recursive builder, easiest to miss)
- ui on both arms of a branch
- a step with ui AND as/spread (ui must not interfere with the as/spread mutual-exclusion check)

*estimate M · depends: S1-T4*

tasks.title: TEST: ui survives the full IR→normalize→toJson round-trip for every step kind incl. nested arms
tasks.body:
The whole point of `ui` is canvas round-trip; the failure mode (schema accepts, normalizer drops) is silent. Build a workflow exercising `ui` on one step of every kind PLUS a step inside each nesting context (branch arm, forEach body, switch case, tryCatch arm). Assert ui is byte-identical after IR → normalizeWorkflow → toJson()→parse. This is the test that catches a missed constructor in T5.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] Round-trip assertion covers all 8 step kinds at top level
- [ ] Round-trip assertion covers ui on a nested step in each of: branch arm, forEach body, switch case, tryCatch arm
- [ ] Test fails if any single normalizer constructor omits the ui copy

**Edge cases to test:**
- passthrough extra key (color) survives
- deeply nested ui (step in forEach inside branch)
- ui absent on most steps, present on one nested one

*estimate S · depends: S1-T5*

tasks.title: Consolidate to one generated schema: $id, checked-in schemas/workflow.v2.json, delete both hand-written VS Code schemas, files:[dist,schemas]
tasks.body:
Extend scripts/build-schema.ts to (1) set $id 'https://schemas.blok.build/workflow/v2.json' (identifier only, need not resolve), (2) ALSO write to core/workflow-helper/schemas/workflow.v2.json (checked in, ships via package), keeping $refStrategy:'none' (verified working — do NOT change). Add `files:['dist','schemas']` (currently ['dist'] — export 404s otherwise) and `exports['./schema']` to package.json. DELETE packages/vscode-extension/schemas/workflow.schema.json (387 lines, stale, the one actually consumed) and workflow-v2.schema.json (176 lines, dead). Point packages/vscode-extension contributes.jsonValidation[].url at a generated copy bundled into the extension's schemas/ dir at build time (VS Code can't resolve a node exports path). Regenerate the schema after T2/T4 so schemaVersion+ui are present.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] schemas/workflow.v2.json generated, checked in, carries $id + schemaVersion + ui for all step kinds
- [ ] package.json has files:['dist','schemas'] and exports['./schema']
- [ ] Both VS Code hand-written schemas deleted; extension points at the generated artifact
- [ ] $refStrategy stays 'none'; generator exits 0

**Edge cases to test:**
- the z.lazy recursive kinds (forEach/loop/switch/tryCatch) still emit fully with 'none' after ui added
- package publish dry-run includes schemas/ (files array honored)
- VS Code validation of a real .json workflow with ui + schemaVersion shows no false errors

*estimate M · depends: S1-T2, S1-T4*

tasks.title: TEST: anti-drift equality (checked-in schema == freshly generated) + completeness assertion
tasks.body:
Extend tests/schemas.test.ts: regenerate via zodToJsonSchema(WorkflowIRSchema, {name:'BlokWorkflowV2', target:'jsonSchema7', $refStrategy:'none'}), read schemas/workflow.v2.json, assert deep-equal after normalize() strips the $id/title/description wrapper. This fails the build if anyone hand-edits the schema or forgets to regenerate after a schema change. Add a completeness assertion: the generated schema contains all 8 step kinds and the ui+schemaVersion properties (guards against a future $refStrategy regression silently dropping recursive kinds).

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] Equality test fails if schemas/workflow.v2.json diverges from the generator
- [ ] Completeness test asserts forEach/loop/switch/tryCatch + ui + schemaVersion all present in the generated output
- [ ] Test is wired into the helper package's `test` script (CI catches drift)

**Edge cases to test:**
- someone edits a .describe() string → equality must catch it
- someone changes $refStrategy → completeness must catch dropped kinds
- the bundled VS Code copy diverging from the canonical schemas/workflow.v2.json (assert they match too)

*estimate S · depends: S1-T7*

tasks.title: Shared validateWorkflow() — ADVISORY only, with explicit v1 detection (not strict-reject)
tasks.body:
Add core/workflow-helper/src/ir/validate.ts: `validateWorkflow(input): {ok:true; workflow} | {ok:false; errors:[{path,message}]}` via WorkflowIRSchema.safeParse, mapping issues to {path:i.path.join('.'), message}. Export from index. CRITICAL constraint from the JSON-path finding: validateWorkflow() must structurally DETECT v1 (steps[] with a sibling nodes{} map, or steps[].name without steps[].id) and return a distinct {ok:false, legacy:true} verdict rather than dumping strict-mode noise — because the JSON load path (scanWorkflows.ts) does NOT Zod-validate today, so validateWorkflow() is a NEW gate and must be advisory (registry-publish + Studio rendering hint + blokctl lint), NOT wired into scanWorkflows load. Document loudly that enforcing it on load is a breaking change deferred to a future schemaVersion bump.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] validateWorkflow returns ok:true + typed workflow for valid v2 input
- [ ] validateWorkflow returns ok:false + path/message errors for invalid v2
- [ ] A v1 (steps[]/nodes{}) input returns a distinct legacy verdict, NOT a wall of strict errors
- [ ] Exported from @blokjs/helper; usable by blokctl and the registry gate by direct import
- [ ] scanWorkflows.ts is NOT modified to enforce it (no load-path behavior change)

**Edge cases to test:**
- v1 file (steps[].name + nodes{})
- _blokV2 builder envelope vs bare _config vs raw JSON object — all three should be acceptable inputs or clearly rejected
- middleware-only workflow with no trigger
- a workflow with a `js/...` string in inputs (valid — must not be flagged)
- deeply nested invalid step (error path points at the right step index)

*estimate M · depends: S1-T2*

tasks.title: TEST: validateWorkflow against the full repo workflow corpus — assert NO regression vs what loads today
tasks.body:
The dangerous claim is 'single validation source everywhere.' Prove validateWorkflow() does not reject anything that scanWorkflows currently loads-and-runs. Enumerate every TS-compiled _config and every JSON workflow under triggers/http. For each that the normalizer accepts today, assert validateWorkflow() either ok:true OR the distinct legacy verdict (never an unexplained ok:false). Separately, assert known-bad inputs (missing id, both as+spread, empty steps, schemaVersion:'3') return ok:false with a useful path. This corpus test is the gate that proves making the validator mandatory would/wouldn't be breaking — it quantifies the gap.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] Every currently-loadable workflow in the repo passes validateWorkflow() (ok:true or legacy), with a printed list of any that don't
- [ ] Known-bad fixtures (missing id, as+spread, empty steps, bad schemaVersion, unknown strict field) return ok:false with correct error path
- [ ] The test output quantifies the v2-strict vs normalizer-tolerant gap (count of files that would break if enforced)

**Edge cases to test:**
- a JSON workflow carrying an extra field the normalizer ignores but .strict() rejects — this is the breaking case to surface
- a workflow using as/spread legitimately
- forEach/switch/tryCatch nesting validated to full depth
- $.state and js/ expressions as input strings — must pass

*estimate M · depends: S1-T9*

tasks.title: SPIKE: Studio @blokjs/helper bundle-size impact (Zod + recursive schema graph) before adopting client-side validateWorkflow
tasks.body:
§7.5(a) adds @blokjs/helper as a Studio dep so Studio imports validateWorkflow directly — verified Studio has NO such dep today. This ships Zod + the full StepOpts schema graph (incl. recursive z.lazy kinds) into the browser bundle. Measure the actual bundle delta (build Studio with the dep + a single validateWorkflow import, diff the bundle). If the delta is unacceptable, the fallback is a server-side /validate endpoint (§7.5b) — which is a real endpoint to design and belongs in S4, NOT S1. Output: measured KB delta + a go/no-go on client-side validation for S1, defaulting to deferring Studio adoption to S4 if the number is bad.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] Measured bundle-size delta (KB, gzipped) of adding @blokjs/helper + validateWorkflow to Studio
- [ ] A go/no-go recommendation: client-side (S1) vs server endpoint (S4)
- [ ] If no-go: Studio adoption explicitly deferred to S4, with the validator still shipped for blokctl/registry in S1

**Edge cases to test:**
- tree-shaking — does importing only validateWorkflow drag in the trigger schemas / proxy code?
- Zod already present transitively in Studio's tree (would make the delta near-zero)
- the recursive z.lazy schemas defeating dead-code elimination

*estimate S · depends: S1-T9*

tasks.title: DOCS: correct the IR contract — `$ref` resolution reality, JSON-path-never-validated, and the validator's advisory scope
tasks.body:
Update S1 spec + the redesign deck data (specs/blok-vision/deck/redesign-data.json has 11 `$ref` mentions claiming 'same Mapper, identical runtime') to reflect T1's decision: state precisely whether the runtime IR carries `{ $ref }` objects (mapper changed) or lowered js-strings (mapper unchanged). Document that the JSON load path (scanWorkflows.ts) does NOT Zod-validate today and that validateWorkflow() is advisory, not a load gate. Fix the factual error that zod-to-json-schema is a 'dependency' (it's a devDependency). Note the 18 .strict() schemas + 8 normalizer constructors as the ui/schemaVersion threading surface so S2/S4 don't re-discover it.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] Spec + deck no longer claim byte-identical mapper resolution of structured `{ $ref }` unless T1 chose the js-string lowering (in which case it's stated as such)
- [ ] Spec records that JSON workflows are not Zod-validated on load and validateWorkflow is advisory
- [ ] zod-to-json-schema corrected to devDependency
- [ ] The .strict()-count and constructor-count threading surface documented for downstream specs

*estimate S · depends: S1-T1, S1-T9*

tasks.title: [gap] TEST: full-corpus resolved-IR + run-trace equivalence gate (the 'nothing lost' proof)
tasks.body:
Build one harness that loads EVERY existing workflow (all TS under triggers/http/src/workflows/ and all JSON under triggers/http/workflows/json/, ~1611 TS + 545 JSON) through both the current loader and the redesigned loader, and asserts (a) the normalized/resolved IR is structurally equal and (b) a representative execution (mocked nodes via WorkflowTestRunner) produces an identical run trace. This is the holistic 'engine byte-identical' proof the per-piece tests do not give. Gate the whole redesign on green here.

**Edge cases:**
- v1 legacy workflows (set_var-free, name+nodes shape) still normalize identically
- JSON file with an extra field the normalizer ignores but a stricter path would reject
- workflow with @blokjs/expr (raw IIFE) — trace must be identical, expression untouched
- forEach/branch/switch/tryCatch nesting — inner-step ids and resolved inputs match
- spread/as/ephemeral steps — state keys land in the same place pre/post

epics.title: Control-flow over handles
epics.body:
The proposed handle-based control-flow surface is sound in DIRECTION but rests on three engine-level holes that the new authoring sugar does not close and in two cases makes WORSE by hiding ids. (1) Duplicate step-ids across branch/switch/tryCatch/forEach arms silently collide in the flat per-workflow config map (`innerNodes[regularStep.name] = nodeConfig` in WorkflowNormalizer; the matched arm runs the OTHER arm's inputs) — there is a test literally titled "Bug 3 — real cause is duplicate inner ids", so this is known and unfixed. Handles make ids feel auto-generated, which will MASS-produce collisions unless the new layer either auto-uniquifies or hard-throws on duplicates. (2) `branch.when` is still evaluated RAW by the if-else node (`nodes/control-flow/if-else@1.0.0/index.ts:24` Function() with NO `js/` strip) — the S3 fix has NOT shipped. The claim "gt/eq/lt return TYPED ops not strings" is the right fix, but a boolean HANDLE (`x.field` truthy) must compile to a raw-ctx string the un-fixed node can eval, or it 500s exactly like the documented `branch when raw ctx` footgun. (3) forEach's per-item handle aliases to `ctx.state[as]` (ForEachNode.cloneCtxForIteration), which shares ONE flat namespace with every sibling step id — a per-item handle named `item` collides with a step `id: "item"`, and the item state silently leaks INTO the iteration's view of sibling state. tryCatch's `$.error` handle is the soundest piece (toErrorEnvelope walks the cause chain, types message/name/stack/code/stepId), but `code`/`stepId` are OPTIONAL and the proposed typed `$.error` handle must model them as optional or authors get `undefined`-at-runtime under a non-optional type. The engine-byte-identical claim holds ONLY for tryCatch/switch/forEach state semantics; it is FALSE for branch (raw eval, unshipped fix) and for the duplicate-id contract (no engine enforcement exists).

**Verdict:** `sound-with-risks`

**Risks / hard cases found in validation:**
- DUPLICATE-ID COLLISION (critical, known-unfixed): WorkflowNormalizer.normalizeBranchStep builds `innerNodes` as a flat Record keyed by step name; duplicate ids across then/else arms (and switch cases, tryCatch try/catch, forEach bodies) overwrite silently — last write wins. The matched arm executes with the OTHER arm's inputs. Existing test foreach-switch-state.test.ts:100 is titled 'Bug 3 — real cause is duplicate inner ids'. Handles that auto-derive ids from variable names will mass-produce this. No validation throws today.
- BRANCH RAW-EVAL FOOTGUN STILL LIVE (critical): the if-else node (nodes/control-flow/if-else@1.0.0/index.ts:17-25,74) compiles `condition.condition` via `Function('ctx',...)` with NO js/ strip. A boolean handle `branch(x.field)` MUST emit a bare-ctx string (e.g. `ctx.state.x.field`), NOT a `js/ctx...` string — otherwise it throws `ReferenceError: js is not defined` inside the node (the exact documented bug). The gt/eq/lt helpers already sidestep this by reading JS_EXPR_TAG raw + canonicalizing aliases; the new boolean-handle path needs the SAME raw emission, and there is no test asserting a bare boolean handle resolves.
- FOREACH ITEM-HANDLE NAMESPACE COLLISION: cloneCtxForIteration sets `state[as] = item` and `state[as+'Index'] = i` into the SAME flat state object shared with all step ids. A per-item handle scoped as `item` collides with any sibling/outer step `id: 'item'` (and `itemIndex` collides with a step `id: 'itemIndex'`). The forEach() factory validates `as` is a JS identifier but does NOT check it against surrounding step ids. The typed per-item handle gives a false sense of scope isolation — it is plain state-key shadowing, reversible by a later same-name step.
- HANDLE ESCAPING ITS BRANCH ARM (soundness, type-vs-runtime divergence): a handle produced by a step inside `then` is type-visible to code in `else` or after the branch, but at RUNTIME that step never ran, so `state[id]` is undefined. Same for switch cases and tryCatch try-arm handles read after the block. The structural `{$ref}` records the path unconditionally; nothing proves the referenced step executed on the taken path. The current engine's truthful `ctx.state[id] === undefined` check (PersistenceHelper Rule 0) is the only guard — the handle type system claims a value that may not exist.
- TRYCATCH ERROR-ENVELOPE OPTIONALITY: toErrorEnvelope (TryCatchNode.ts:67-133) types message/name as always-present but code/stepId as conditional (`...meta` only spreads when defined). A non-GlobalError throw yields code:undefined; a pre-wrap throw yields stepId:undefined. The proposed typed `$.error` handle must model code?/stepId? as OPTIONAL or authors reading `error.code` get a non-optional type masking a runtime undefined. Also: WaitDispatchRequest/RunCancelledError are re-thrown past catch (TryCatchNode.ts:195) — a handle author who expects catch to fire on 'any throw' is wrong for these two control signals.
- ENGINE-BYTE-IDENTICAL CLAIM IS PARTIALLY FALSE: holds for tryCatch/switch/forEach STATE persistence (all route through applyStepOutput unchanged). FALSE for branch (raw eval path, S3 fix unshipped) and FALSE for duplicate-id safety (no engine enforcement exists at all — the new layer must ADD it, which is a behavior change, not a no-op).
- LOOP CONDITION SCOPE DIVERGENCE: LoopNode.evaluateCondition binds only ctx,data,vars (NO func) and strips no js/ prefix (per S3 §2 table). A loop `while` handle/op must emit raw-ctx like branch; additionally a handle referencing `func.*` resolves in step inputs but ReferenceErrors in a loop condition. Divergent eval sites mean a handle that works in `inputs` can break in `while`.
- SWITCH `on` VS `case.when` ASYMMETRY: SwitchNode resolves `on` via mapper (handle-friendly) but matches `case.when` by literal `===`/`includes` with NO resolution. A typed switch where a case value is itself a handle will compare against the unresolved js/-string, never matching. The switchOn() factory unwrapProxies the `when`, turning a handle into a js/-string literal that === never equals the resolved `on`.

**Open questions to resolve:**
- Duplicate-id policy: hard-THROW at load time on any duplicate id within a workflow (including across arms), or auto-uniquify by suffixing arm path (e.g. `route.then.create`)? Throw is safer + matches the standing 'framework bug → fail loud' rule; auto-uniquify is friendlier to handle-based authoring where the author may legitimately reuse a name in mutually-exclusive arms. Recommend THROW with a hint to use `as:` for shared downstream keys.
- Boolean-handle compilation target: should a bare boolean handle `branch(x.field)` compile to `ctx.state.x.field` (raw, evals truthy) AND emit the S3 build-time warn-nudge that it's a truthiness check, or REQUIRE a gt/eq/lt op for non-boolean-typed handles? The type system CAN distinguish a `z.boolean()` handle from others — recommend allowing bare boolean handles only when the handle's Zod type is boolean, forcing ops otherwise.
- Does the new layer ship ON TOP of the unshipped S3 Mapper-routing fix, or independently? If branch/loop conditions still hit the raw if-else/LoopNode eval, the handle layer must emit raw-ctx strings AND the S3 fix is a hard prerequisite for any handle that isn't a literal comparison. Sequencing matters: S3 M1 (resolveCondition) should land first.
- forEach item-handle scope: enforce that `as` cannot equal any surrounding step id (load-time throw), or make item-handles a genuinely separate namespace (e.g. `ctx.loop[as]` instead of `ctx.state[as]`) so they CAN'T collide? The latter is an engine change (breaks the byte-identical claim) but is the only real fix for the false-isolation impression handles create.
- Handle-escapes-arm: should reading a handle outside its proving branch arm be a TYPE error (track arm-scope in the handle type), or remain a runtime-undefined the author must guard? Type-level arm scoping is a large TS undertaking; the lazy answer is runtime-undefined + a documented `state[id] === undefined` check, but that undercuts the 'typed handle' selling point.

tasks.title: Load-time duplicate-id detection across all control-flow arms
tasks.body:
In WorkflowNormalizer, collect every step `id` across the full tree (top-level + branch then/else, switch cases+default, tryCatch try/catch/finally, forEach/loop bodies) and THROW a structured error on any duplicate, naming both occurrences and their arm paths. Today `innerNodes[regularStep.name] = nodeConfig` (normalizeBranchStep, ~WorkflowNormalizer.ts:485/506) silently overwrites — the matched arm runs the wrong inputs. Error message must hint: 'use `as:` to write a shared downstream key from mutually-exclusive arms'. This is the engine enforcement the handle layer needs but the engine does not yet provide.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] A workflow with two steps `id:'create'` in then and else throws at load with both arm paths named
- [ ] A duplicate id between a top-level step and a nested arm step throws
- [ ] Legitimately-distinct ids across arms still load
- [ ] The fix lives in the normalizer so JSON-authored AND handle-authored workflows are both protected

**Edge cases to test:**
- duplicate id in tryCatch try vs finally
- duplicate id forEach body vs outer sibling
- switch case A and case B both use id 'h' (the CLAUDE.md switch example literally does this — that example must be updated)
- deeply nested branch-in-catch-in-forEach

*estimate M*

tasks.title: TEST: duplicate-id collision + wrong-arm-inputs regression
tasks.body:
Promote/extend foreach-switch-state.test.ts (the 'Bug 3 — real cause is duplicate inner ids' test) into a full regression suite proving CF-1. Must include the FAILING-WITHOUT-FIX case: two arms with the same id where the matched arm currently runs the other arm's inputs (assert the corrupt behavior is now a load-time throw, not silent execution). Cover branch, switch, tryCatch, forEach, and cross-level collisions.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] Test reproduces the pre-fix silent wrong-inputs execution and asserts it now throws
- [ ] All five primitive arm-types covered
- [ ] A passing test for legitimate distinct ids (no false positive)
- [ ] Test for the `as:`-rename escape hatch resolving the collision legitimately

**Edge cases to test:**
- same id in three+ arms
- id collision only after normalization (v1→v2 name→id mapping)
- case-only difference in ids (should NOT collide)

*estimate M · depends: CF-1*

tasks.title: Boolean/op handle → raw-ctx condition string for branch & loop
tasks.body:
Make the handle-based `branch(handle | op)` and `loop(handle | op)` compile their condition to a BARE ctx string (e.g. `ctx.state.x.field`, `ctx.state.count > 10`) — NOT a `js/ctx...` string — because the if-else node (nodes/control-flow/if-else@1.0.0/index.ts:24) and LoopNode evaluate raw via Function() with no js/ strip. Reuse the eq.ts pattern (read JS_EXPR_TAG, canonicalizeCtxPath for req/prev/vars aliases). A bare boolean handle emits the path as a truthiness check; gt/eq/lt/etc emit `<path> <op> <literal>`. If the S3 Mapper-routing fix lands first, switch to emitting `js/`-prefixed and let resolveCondition strip it — but until then, raw is mandatory.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] `branch(x.field)` where x is a boolean handle compiles to `ctx.state.<id>.field` and evals truthy at runtime without ReferenceError
- [ ] `branch(gt(x.count, 10))` compiles to `ctx.state.<id>.count > 10`
- [ ] alias canonicalization: a req/prev/vars-rooted handle maps to request/response/state
- [ ] loop `while` handle gets identical treatment incl. the counter `<id>Index`

**Edge cases to test:**
- handle on a deeply-nested path with bracket access
- negation of a boolean handle
- comparison where BOTH operands are handles
- handle rooted at $.error inside a catch-arm branch

*estimate M*

tasks.title: TEST: boolean/op handle conditions resolve without raw-eval 500
tasks.body:
Run actual branch/loop workflows (WorkflowTestRunner + a real if-else/LoopNode execution, not just string-compilation assertions — the documented bug is that typecheck + WorkflowTestRunner MISS the raw-ctx 500, so the test MUST execute the node) proving every handle/op form evaluates correctly. Include the negative test that a naive `js/ctx...` emission throws ReferenceError inside the node — locking in why raw emission is required.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] Bare boolean handle routes to `then` when truthy, `else` when falsy, executed end-to-end
- [ ] gt/lt/eq/ne/gte/lte handle ops each route correctly
- [ ] A js/-prefixed condition is asserted to throw inside if-else (guards against regression to the footgun)
- [ ] loop counter handle (`<id>Index < N`) terminates correctly and hits maxIterations cap

**Edge cases to test:**
- condition referencing a forEach item handle inside the loop body
- condition that throws at eval (assert MapperResolutionError vs garbled crash per BLOK_MAPPER_MODE)
- empty/whitespace condition rejected at build

*estimate M · depends: CF-3*

tasks.title: forEach item-handle: prevent `as` colliding with surrounding step ids
tasks.body:
ForEachNode.cloneCtxForIteration writes `state[as]` and `state[as+'Index']` into the flat state shared with all step ids — a per-item handle named `item` collides with a sibling/outer step `id:'item'` and silently shadows it. Add load-time validation (in forEach() factory AND the normalizer for JSON authors) that `as` and `as+'Index'` do not equal any surrounding-scope step id. Decide with the founder (open question) whether to additionally move item state to a separate namespace; minimum bar is the collision check + clear error.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] forEach with `as:'item'` and a sibling `id:'item'` throws at load with a clear message
- [ ] `as:'x'` with a step `id:'xIndex'` is also caught (the Index suffix)
- [ ] non-colliding `as` loads fine
- [ ] validation runs for both TS-factory and JSON-authored forEach

**Edge cases to test:**
- nested forEach where inner `as` collides with outer `as`
- `as` collides with an id in a DIFFERENT arm (mutually exclusive — is that a real collision? document the decision)
- parallel-mode iterations: confirm per-iteration clone still isolates item state across workers (it deep-clones — assert no bleed)

*estimate M*

tasks.title: TEST: forEach item-handle scope isolation + collision
tasks.body:
Prove (a) per-item handle does NOT bleed across iterations (sequential AND parallel — cloneCtxForIteration deep-clones config + forks state), (b) a later same-name step inside the body shadows then is reverted next iteration, (c) the CF-5 collision check fires. Parallel mode is the high-risk path: assert two iterations mutating `state[as].x` don't see each other's writes.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] Sequential: iteration N's item handle is item N, not N-1's leftover
- [ ] Parallel mode with concurrency>1: no cross-iteration item bleed
- [ ] Collision check throws on `as` == sibling id
- [ ] `<as>Index` is correct 0-based per iteration in both modes

**Edge cases to test:**
- forEach over empty array (item handle never bound — downstream read is the no-op state[]=[] path)
- item handle read AFTER the forEach (outside body) — assert it's the loop's aggregated array, not a stale last item
- parallel wait-resume: item handle correct after a mid-iteration wait re-entry

*estimate L · depends: CF-5*

tasks.title: Typed `$.error` handle modeling code?/stepId? as optional + control-signal carve-out
tasks.body:
Define the tryCatch error handle type to mirror toErrorEnvelope (TryCatchNode.ts:67-133) EXACTLY: message:string, name:string, stack?:string, code?:number, stepId?:string — code and stepId MUST be optional (a non-GlobalError throw yields code:undefined; a pre-wrap throw yields stepId:undefined). The handle must compile `error.message` etc. to `ctx.error.message` (the runtime field the mapper reads). Document that the catch arm does NOT fire for WaitDispatchRequest/RunCancelledError (re-thrown past catch at TryCatchNode.ts:195) — the handle's 'catches any throw' mental model is wrong for these two.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] `error.code` is typed `number | undefined`, not `number`
- [ ] `error.message`/`error.name` are non-optional
- [ ] handle compiles to a raw/mapper-resolvable `ctx.error.*` form consumable inside the catch arm
- [ ] docs note the wait/cancel carve-out

**Edge cases to test:**
- reading `$.error` in try or finally (undefined — TryCatchNode restores priorError around finally; assert handle reflects this)
- nested tryCatch: inner catch's error vs outer (priorError snapshot/restore)
- thrown non-Error value (string/number) — name defaults to 'Error', message is String(value)

*estimate S*

tasks.title: TEST: $.error handle envelope fidelity across throw shapes
tasks.body:
Extend tryCatch.test.ts to assert the typed handle resolves correctly for: plain Error, GlobalError with code, @blokjs/throw with code, ZodError→400, deeply-wrapped cause chain (message is the ROOT, not the framework `[step N/M]` wrap), and stepId presence. Assert WaitDispatchRequest/RunCancelledError thrown in `try` are NOT caught (re-thrown, catch arm does not run, finally still runs per the contract).

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] code surfaces from GlobalError.context.code for the first GlobalError in the chain
- [ ] stepId surfaces from _blokStepId on the wrap layer
- [ ] message is the deepest cause, stripped of framework prefix
- [ ] wait/cancel signal in try bypasses catch and re-throws

**Edge cases to test:**
- error with circular .cause (the walk guards `cause !== unwrapped` — assert no infinite loop)
- catch arm itself throws (pendingError propagates after finally)
- finally throws (supersedes catch's pendingError — JS semantics)

*estimate M · depends: CF-7*

tasks.title: Switch typed `on`/`case.when` consistency — resolve or forbid handle case values
tasks.body:
SwitchNode resolves `on` via the mapper but matches `case.when` by literal `===`/includes with NO resolution (SwitchNode.ts:60-65). A typed switch where a `when` is a handle gets unwrapProxies'd to a `js/...` string literal (switchOn.ts:106) that `=== on` never equals. Either (a) forbid handles in `case.when` at build time with a clear error (case values should be literals), or (b) resolve case.when through the mapper before comparison. Recommend (a) — case values being literals matches the discriminated-union mental model and avoids a second eval site.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] A handle passed as `case.when` throws a clear build-time error directing the author to a literal
- [ ] literal scalar and literal-array `when` still work
- [ ] the `on` handle continues to resolve via mapper unchanged

**Edge cases to test:**
- array `when` containing a handle element
- `on` resolves to a type that never === any literal case (falls to default)
- switch resume path (cursor pins caseIndex, ignores `on`) unaffected

*estimate S*

tasks.title: SPIKE: handle-escapes-arm — type-level arm scoping vs runtime-undefined guard
tasks.body:
Determine whether a handle produced inside a branch/switch/tryCatch arm can be prevented (at the TYPE level) from being read on a path where its step didn't run, or whether we accept runtime-undefined + a documented `state[id] === undefined` guard. The structural `{$ref}` records the path unconditionally; on the not-taken arm the referenced step never executes so state[id] is undefined (PersistenceHelper Rule 0). Prototype a TS approach (branded arm-scoped handle types) and measure ergonomic cost; compare against the lazy documented-guard option. Output a recommendation, not code.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] A written recommendation with a TS prototype showing whether arm-scoping is feasible without crippling autocomplete
- [ ] Quantified ergonomic/complexity cost of the type-level option
- [ ] Fallback documented-guard wording if type-level is rejected
- [ ] Covers branch then/else, switch case→sibling, tryCatch try-handle-read-in-catch

**Edge cases to test:**
- handle from a step that ran but was ephemeral (state[id] undefined even on the taken path)
- handle read after the whole branch (legitimately may-or-may-not exist)
- forEach body handle read outside the loop

*estimate M*

tasks.title: DOCS: control-flow handle contract + the four footguns
tasks.body:
Single page documenting the handle-based control-flow surface and the sharp edges this validation surfaced: (1) ids must be unique workflow-wide incl. across arms (CF-1) — use `as:` for shared downstream keys; (2) boolean handles in branch/loop are truthiness checks, use gt/eq/lt for comparisons (CF-3); (3) forEach `as` shares the state namespace — don't name it after a step id (CF-5); (4) `$.error` code/stepId are optional, and catch does NOT fire for wait/cancel signals (CF-7). Sync with the existing 'branch when raw ctx' CLAUDE.md memory entry and the context-and-state fundamentals page.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] All four footguns documented with correct/incorrect examples
- [ ] Links to S3 expression spec for the eval-site divergence
- [ ] CLAUDE.md 'Do NOT' list updated to reflect handle-based authoring
- [ ] switch example with duplicate `id:'h'` across cases corrected

*estimate S · depends: CF-1, CF-3, CF-5, CF-7*

tasks.title: [gap] TEST: forEach iterable-handle, switch.on handle, loop.while handle resolve end-to-end
tasks.body:
Cover the control-flow INPUT expression sites the existing tasks miss: forEach(handle, ...) where the iterable is a handle (mapper-resolved), switch on: handle with case.when literal-match asymmetry, and loop while: handle/op at the divergent eval site that binds ctx,data,vars but NOT func. Assert each resolves without a raw-eval 500 and that a func.* reference in a while condition is rejected loudly rather than silently ReferenceError-ing.

**Edge cases:**
- forEach over a handle that resolves to non-array (undefined/null/object)
- switch on: handle vs case.when that is itself a handle (must resolve or be forbidden)
- loop while: handle referencing func.* (resolves in inputs, throws in while)
- empty iterable handle — zero iterations, no per-item state leak

epics.title: Adversarial validation: Remove the ctx authoring surface
epics.body:
The handle model is sound for the COMMON case (field reads chained between steps). Every primary author-facing ctx.* form has a structural replacement: ctx.state[id] -> step handle; ctx.prev -> immediately-prior handle; ctx.req/event/job -> typed entry handle; $.error -> per-tryCatch.catch error handle; forEach item -> per-item handle. BUT the "ctx becomes internal" framing is half-true and dangerous as written, on two hard walls. (1) defineNode.execute(ctx, input) at core/runner/src/defineNode.ts:76 hands EVERY node the full Context. ctx is the node ABI, not an author surface; ctx.publish, ctx.signal, ctx.connection, ctx.stream, ctx.logger are deliberately node-side. The epic conflates the authoring surface (workflow files) with the node surface (defineNode) -- only the former is removable; the latter MUST keep ctx. (2) Real authored workflows are saturated with arbitrary JS expressions a structural {$ref} cannot encode: "js/ctx.request.body.tenantId || 'default'", "body.action || null", "body.commits || []" appear at dozens of sites in triggers/http/src/workflows/examples/*.ts. A purely structural handle drops the "|| default", arithmetic, .filter/.map, and string-building beyond tpl. Without a defined escape hatch (a js/ literal handle, an expr() helper, or a compute node) the new DSL is strictly LESS expressive than today and these workflows cannot be ported. Also branch when already does NOT flow through the mapper -- it is a raw Function(ctx) eval (eq.ts comment + understand-mapper.md invariant #7), and gt/eq emit raw "ctx.* op literal" strings -- so "no ctx in authoring" already has a known carve-out the boolean-handle design must preserve. Verdict: sound core that leaks ctx in four named places and silently loses expressiveness. Bulletproof requires scoping "remove ctx" to author files only, naming the node-side ctx members as out of scope, and shipping a typed escape hatch for the non-structural expressions before any author-facing parity claim.

**Verdict:** `sound-with-risks`

**Risks / hard cases found in validation:**
- EXPRESSIVENESS LOSS (highest): triggers/http/src/workflows/examples/*.ts use arbitrary exprs the structural {$ref} cannot encode -- 'js/ctx.request.body.tenantId || 1default1', 'body.action || null', 'body.commits || []' (coalescing/defaulting), plus implied arithmetic/.filter/.map/concat. A handle that only records {$ref:{step,path}} loses these. tpl covers string interpolation only. Without a defined escape hatch the DSL is strictly less expressive than today; dozens of real workflows cannot round-trip.
- CTX IS THE NODE ABI, NOT REMOVABLE: defineNode.execute(ctx, input) (core/runner/src/defineNode.ts:76) passes full Context to every node. ctx.publish (TriggerBase.ts:1767, createChildContext.ts:128), ctx.signal (Context.ts:126), ctx.connection (ConnectionContext, WebSocket), ctx.stream (StreamContext, SSE) are node-side APIs with NO handle equivalent and no business being one. The epic says ctx 'becomes internal' -- true for AUTHOR files, false for nodes. If scope is not stated precisely, implementers will try to strip ctx from defineNode and break every cross-runtime node, ws-reply, sse-stream, and cancellation-aware node.
- BRANCH WHEN BYPASSES THE MAPPER/HANDLE PATH: branch when is a raw Function(ctx) eval, NOT mapper-resolved (understand-mapper.md invariant #7; eq.ts header comment). gt/eq/ne/lt emit raw 'ctx.* op literal' strings (eq.ts). So a 'boolean handle' for branch must compile to that raw-ctx form, not a {$ref}. If the new branch(handle) naively emits {$ref} it will 500 at runtime exactly like the documented branch-when-raw-ctx footgun in MEMORY. The two condition paths (mapper inputs vs raw-ctx branch when) must stay distinct or be unified deliberately with a migration.
- CROSS-RUNTIME vars_delta HAS NO HANDLE: cross-runtime nodes publish extra state via the proto vars_delta field, merged in RuntimeAdapterNode.ts:111-115 (Object.assign(state, result.vars)). Those keys are dynamic, decided at runtime by the sidecar, unknown to the TS type system. A typed handle shaped from the node Zod OUTPUT cannot reference them (they are not in output). Either vars_delta keys must be declared in the manifest/output schema, or authors lose typed access to side-channel-published cross-runtime values.
- SPREAD AND AS BREAK HANDLE IDENTITY: with spread:true the result.data keys merge into state at top level (state.user not state.<id>.user); with as:'name' the result lands at state[name]. The handle returned by step() is shaped from the node output and keyed by id -- but the actual state key changes. forEach 'as' similarly writes state[as] and state[as+'Index'] (forEach.ts). If step() returns one handle but spread/as relocate the data, reads via the handle resolve to the wrong/absent state path. The {$ref} the handle emits must be computed AFTER knowing spread/as, not at step() call time.
- PUBLISH SIDE-CHANNEL VALUES ARE UNTYPED + UNREFERENCEABLE: a node calling ctx.publish(name, value) writes an arbitrary runtime-named key into state that no compile-time handle knows about. Today authors reach it via $.state.<name>. In a handle-only world there is no handle for a name the type system never saw. Need an escape hatch (state('name') typed-as-unknown accessor) or published values become unreadable from authoring.
- IDEMPOTENCY/CONCURRENCY/SUBWORKFLOW KEYS ARE EXPRESSION SITES TOO: idempotencyKey, concurrencyKey, and polymorphic subworkflow (StepOpts.ts; webhook-stripe.ts uses subworkflow:'js/ctx.request.body.type') all accept $.<path> / js/... expressions resolved by separate resolvers (resolveIdempotencyKey.ts custom eval, SubworkflowNode.resolveSubworkflowName). These are NOT step inputs and do not flow through the same mapper. The handle model must define how a handle is accepted here AND that fail-open vs fail-strict asymmetry (idempotency keys fail open to null, mapper fails strict) is preserved.
- ADJACENCY (ctx.prev) IS POSITIONAL, HANDLES ARE NOT: ctx.prev is 'the immediately previous step output, overwritten every step'. A handle is a stable named reference. They are not equivalent: inside a branch/forEach/tryCatch arm, 'previous' depends on control flow and nesting depth. Mapping ctx.prev to 'the prior step handle' is ambiguous at arm boundaries (what is prev for the first step in an else arm?). Either ban prev-style adjacency in the new DSL (force explicit handles) or define adjacency semantics across nesting precisely.

**Open questions to resolve:**
- What is the sanctioned escape hatch for non-structural expressions (|| default, arithmetic, .filter/.map, concat beyond tpl)? Options: a js`...` tagged-template handle that emits a raw js/ literal (lowest effort, reuses existing mapper), an expr(fn) helper, or forcing a @blokjs/expr compute node. The founder must pick ONE before parity can be claimed.
- Is ctx formally OUT OF SCOPE inside defineNode? The epic says defineNode is UNCHANGED -- confirm that means nodes keep full ctx and only WORKFLOW FILES lose it. If yes, the epic title 'ctx.state/prev/vars/response/request become internal' should read 'become non-author-facing', and ctx.publish/signal/connection/stream are explicitly retained node-side.
- Does branch unify onto the mapper path, or does the boolean handle keep compiling to the raw-ctx Function form? Unifying is cleaner but is an engine change that contradicts the 'engine byte-identical' claim. Keeping raw-ctx means the handle compiler needs a second emit mode.
- How do authors reference cross-runtime vars_delta keys and ctx.publish names that are not in the node Zod output? Declare them in the manifest, or provide an untyped state('name') accessor?
- Is ctx.prev adjacency dropped entirely in the new DSL (handles only), and if so, what replaces the genuinely-ergonomic 'use the last step's output' pattern inside a single linear pipeline?

tasks.title: SPIKE: define the escape hatch for non-structural expressions
tasks.body:
Inventory every arbitrary (non-field-read) expression in triggers/http/src/workflows/examples/*.ts and any user workflows (grep for js/ctx with ||, +, .map, .filter, .length, ?:, &&). Categorize: coalesce/default, arithmetic, collection ops, string-build. For each category decide the handle-world replacement. Prototype a js`...` tagged template that emits a raw 'js/...' literal preserving today's mapper semantics (Mapper.jsMapper already evaluates js/ strings -- core/shared/src/utils/Mapper.ts). Confirm it round-trips through unwrapProxies/IR without double-evaluation (note @blokjs/expr double-eval footgun in CLAUDE.md). tasks.title: Compute handle {$ref} AFTER resolving spread/as/ephemeral
tasks.body:
step() returns a handle keyed by id, but the actual state location depends on persistence knobs: as:'name' -> state[name]; spread:true -> top-level state keys; ephemeral:true -> NOT in state (only prev). The {$ref} a handle field emits must reflect the final location. Implement so the handle compiler reads the step's resolved knobs and emits $ref:{step:<as||id>, path} for as, top-level $ref for spread members, and a compile-time ERROR when a handle from an ephemeral step is read by a non-adjacent consumer (mirrors today's 'state[X] is undefined' footgun, but caught early). Reuse the existing PersistenceHelper key logic (core/runner/src/workflow/PersistenceHelper.ts:applyStepOutput) as the single source of truth for the key -- do not re-derive it.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] Reading a handle from an as:'name' step resolves to state[name] at runtime
- [ ] Reading a spread member resolves to the top-level state key
- [ ] Reading a handle from an ephemeral step from a non-adjacent step is a COMPILE error, not a runtime undefined
- [ ] Handle key derivation calls/mirrors PersistenceHelper, not a duplicate

**Edge cases to test:**
- spread member name collides with another step id
- as:'run' used on two mutually-exclusive branch arms (CLAUDE.md duplicate-id footgun) -- handles from both arms must resolve to state.run
- spread:true on a cross-runtime node whose data keys are only known from the manifest
- ephemeral step read by the IMMEDIATELY next step (allowed via prev) vs a later step (error)

tasks.body:

Do NOT emit {$ref} for branch when or it 500s (MEMORY: branch-when-raw-ctx). forEach(handle, item => ...): item is a typed per-item handle shaped from the array element; preserve forEach.ts semantics (state[as]=item, state[as+'Index']=i) and ensure the item handle's $ref resolves per-iteration. tryCatch: the catch arm gets a typed error handle replacing $.error -> resolves to ctx.error (only defined inside catch; undefined elsewhere -- enforce at compile time).

**Acceptance:**

tasks.title: Handles at idempotencyKey/concurrencyKey/polymorphic subworkflow sites

tasks.title: SPIKE: ctx.prev adjacency replacement across nesting

Decide what replaces ctx.prev (immediately-previous output, overwritten each step). Options: (1) drop prev entirely, force every read to an explicit step handle (most sound, mild ergonomic cost); (2) keep a special prev() handle with precisely-defined nesting semantics. tasks.title: [gap] Implement + TEST tpl backtick template that keeps refs structural
tasks.body:
Build tpl`...${handle.field}...` so interpolated handle reads stay structural ({$ref} or lowered js/ per the chosen wire format) and literal text stays literal. This is the only sanctioned replacement for string-building js/ expressions; without it a large slice of the corpus is un-migratable. Lower tpl to whatever wire format the SPIKE (ir/corepkg) selects.

**Edge cases:**
- multiple handles in one template
- nested field access ${x.a.b} where x.a may be undefined (strict-mode interplay)
- literal ${ that is NOT a handle (escaping)

epics.title: Adversarial validation: Auto-persist-all + handle resolution
epics.body:
The engine claim holds. PersistenceHelper.applyStepOutput (Rules 0/1/2/3) and Mapper.jsMapper can stay byte-identical. A step() handle that records {$ref:{step,path}} and unwraps to "js/ctx.state.<step>.<path>" is exactly the wire-shape the existing $-proxy already produces (core/workflow-helper/src/proxy/$.ts unwraps "$.state.fetch" to "js/ctx.state.fetch"). So "only the authoring surface changes" is TRUE for the happy path: a handle is a typed $-proxy rooted at a known step id, and the runner needs zero engine changes to resolve it. The adversarial pass found real soundness gaps where the authoring layer promises things the engine cannot keep, because the engine resolves $ref against runtime ctx.state with NO knowledge of the handle graph. Headline hole: reading an ephemeral step via a handle is neither a compile error nor loud today (Rule 1 no-ops the write, so x.field compiles to js/ctx.state.log.field which is an undefined access; strict mode fails at the CONSUMER step with a confusing message, warn/silent silently miscompiles). The mandate explicitly requires loud-or-compile-error, so this is the gating defect. Other gaps: as/spread relocate output away from state[id] so a handle keyed on id resolves to undefined; forEach per-item handles compile to state[as] which is mutated/overwritten and only valid inside the loop body; idempotency replay already routes through applyStepOutput so handles are safe there (verify only); persist-all memory growth on huge workflows needs a measured bound. Verdict: sound provided the handle TYPE SYSTEM makes ephemeral-reads and as/spread mismatches unrepresentable, and a runtime guard turns a missing-state $ref into a named blameable error. Grounded in core/runner/src/workflow/PersistenceHelper.ts, core/shared/src/utils/Mapper.ts, core/workflow-helper/src/proxy/$.ts, core/runner/src/ForEachNode.ts:584-587.

**Verdict:** `sound-with-risks`

**Risks / hard cases found in validation:**
- R1 (GATING) Ephemeral handle read is silent, not loud. step("log", node, {ephemeral:true}) returns a handle; reading x.field unwraps to js/ctx.state.log.field. Rule 1 in PersistenceHelper.ts:64 never wrote state.log, so at runtime it is an undefined access. With the default BLOK_MAPPER_MODE=strict it throws MapperResolutionError at the CONSUMING step (wrong blame target, message mentions the consumer not the author of the bad ref); with warn/silent it passes the literal js/... string through = silent miscompile. Mandate requires compile-error-or-loud; neither exists. Fix must be in the TYPE of the handle: an ephemeral step() must return a handle whose field-access type is never (or a branded EphemeralHandle that has no readable fields).
- R2 as/spread relocate the output away from state[id], breaking the handle's path root. as:"foo" -> applyStepOutput Rule 3 writes state.foo not state.<id>; spread:true -> Rule 2 scatters result.data.{k} into state.{k}. A handle minted from the step id (and unwrapping to js/ctx.state.<id>...) resolves to undefined under either knob. The handle's compiled root MUST be derived from the persistence decision (as ?? id for default/as; the individual spread keys for spread), and spread handles can only be typed if the node output Zod shape is statically known so each top-level key becomes its own readable handle.
- R3 forEach per-item handle is closure-scoped and mutation-based. ForEachNode.ts:586-587 does state[as]=item / state[as+'Index']=i, overwriting every iteration. A typed per-item handle compiles to js/ctx.state.<as>. It is ONLY valid inside the do[] body of that iteration. If an author captures the per-item handle and references it from a step AFTER the forEach, it resolves to the LAST item (sequential) or a torn/racy value (parallel mode clones state per branch, ForEachNode.ts:584 baseState). The type system must scope the per-item handle to the callback closure so it is uncapturable outside; runtime cannot detect the leak.
- R4 Truthful-undefined contract is preserved BUT now ambiguous between two causes. Rule 0 (errored result) and Rule 1 (ephemeral) BOTH leave state[id]===undefined. Under handles, state[id]===undefined inside a tryCatch.catch arm can mean either 'step threw' or 'step was ephemeral' - the author's did-this-succeed check silently lies for ephemeral steps. The handle layer must forbid the existence-check pattern on ephemeral handles (same fix as R1) and document that the check is only meaningful for persisted steps.
- R5 Persist-all memory growth has no measured ceiling. Every successful step writes state[id]; forEach over N items mutates state[as] N times (bounded, good) but a workflow with thousands of sequential persisted steps, or spread steps fanning many keys, grows ctx.state monotonically for the whole run. Long-lived runs (wait/defer/re-entry) keep the whole state object resident and re-serialize it into node_runs and into http-self sub-workflow bodies (capped at BLOK_DISPATCH_PAYLOAD_MAX_BYTES=1MB - a large state can blow this cap, surfacing as a 413 from a place authors do not expect). No automatic GC of state slots no later step references; ephemeral is the only opt-out and authors will not reach for it by default.
- R6 spread+handle requires the node output schema at authoring time, which cross-runtime nodes only have via the generated runtimeNode stub. If the ListNodes JSON-Schema manifest is stale or the node returns extra/missing keys, the spread handle's typed keys diverge from runtime reality - a typed read that resolves to undefined with no warning. Couples handle soundness to blokctl nodes sync freshness.
- R7 Mapper strict-mode default amplifies handle bugs into hard failures across the whole workflow. Any handle that resolves to undefined at a non-leaf path (e.g. x.a.b where x.a is undefined) throws and fails the consuming step. Good for catching typos, but a single mis-rooted handle (R1/R2/R3) becomes a run-killing error rather than a localized one, and the error text points at the mapper expression, not the offending step() call site - poor authoring DX without a source-map back to the handle origin.

**Open questions to resolve:**
- Should an ephemeral step be allowed to return a handle at all, or should step(id, node, {ephemeral:true}) return void (TS error on any read)? Returning a branded EphemeralHandle gives a better error message than void but costs a type.
- For spread:true, do we require the node's Zod output to be a statically-known object so each key becomes a typed sub-handle, and hard-error at authoring if output is z.unknown()/z.record()? Without that, spread handles cannot be sound.
- Do we add a thin runtime guard in the Mapper (or a pre-flight) that, when a js/ctx.state.<id> ref resolves to undefined, throws a NamedMissingStateError carrying the step id and the referencing step - turning R1/R2/R3 runtime failures from generic undefined-access into blameable errors? This is a small additive engine change, contradicting the strict 'zero engine change' claim but only on the error-message path.
- Should the IR carry the {$ref:{step,path}} structurally (not pre-compiled to js/ strings) so the canvas and AI can validate dangling refs statically, with the js/ compilation happening only at the runner boundary? The mandate says the DSL compiles to js/-shaped inputs today via unwrapProxies; keeping $ref structural in the IR is a better validation substrate but is a divergence to decide now.
- What is the acceptable upper bound on ctx.state size for a single run, and do we want an opt-in state-slot GC (drop slots no downstream step references, computable from the handle graph at compile time) before this ships at scale?

tasks.title: Handle type: ephemeral steps return an unreadable handle
tasks.body:
In the new step() builder, type the return so that a step created with {ephemeral:true} yields a handle whose field access is a compile error. Implement as a branded EphemeralHandle<T> whose indexable members resolve to never (or omit the readable proxy entirely), while still being passable where a step ordering token is needed. Root the handle's compiled path on (as ?? id). Mint the {$ref:{step,path}} only for persisted handles. Ground: mirrors core/workflow-helper/src/proxy/$.ts makeProxy/unwrapProxies which already produce js/ctx.state.<id> strings.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] A persisted step() handle, when read (x.field), unwraps to "js/ctx.state.<as??id>.field"
- [ ] An ephemeral step() handle read (x.field) is a TypeScript compile error
- [ ] The compiled inputs for a persisted handle are byte-identical to today's $.state.<id> unwrap output (snapshot test against proxy/$.ts)
- [ ] applyStepOutput, Mapper, RunnerSteps are UNCHANGED (no engine diff in this task)

**Edge cases to test:**
- ephemeral handle passed as a pure ordering dependency (allowed) vs read (error)
- handle with as:"foo" reads as state.foo not state.<id>
- handle field that is itself optional in the node Zod output
- nested path x.a.b.c builds the correct ["a"]["b"]["c"] / .a.b.c compiled string
- numeric index x[0] and non-identifier key x["weird-key"] compile correctly (NUMERIC_RE / JSON.stringify branches in $.ts)

*estimate L*

tasks.title: TEST: ephemeral + as/spread handle soundness (compile + runtime)
tasks.body:
Adversarial test suite for HANDLE-1 and HANDLE-3. Use tsd/expect-error (or @ts-expect-error blocks compiled in CI) for the compile-time contracts and WorkflowTestRunner for the runtime contracts. Every risk R1/R2/R4 gets a failing-by-default case.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] @ts-expect-error: reading any field off an ephemeral handle fails typecheck
- [ ] Runtime: a persisted handle resolves to the step output; an ephemeral step is absent from ctx.state (assert state[id]===undefined) confirming Rule 1 still no-ops
- [ ] as:"foo" handle resolves to state.foo at runtime; a handle still rooted at id would resolve undefined (negative control)
- [ ] tryCatch.catch arm: state[id]===undefined is asserted to be ambiguous between throw and ephemeral, and the ephemeral path is blocked at compile time so the ambiguity is unreachable
- [ ] Snapshot: compiled js/ string for a multi-segment handle equals the legacy $-proxy output

**Edge cases to test:**
- ephemeral step read attempted across a tryCatch boundary
- handle read before the step is declared (forward ref) - compile order
- as and spread both set (must remain the mutually-exclusive load-time error)

*estimate M · depends: HANDLE-1, HANDLE-3*

tasks.title: Spread handle: per-key typed sub-handles from node output schema
tasks.body:
For step(id, node, {spread:true}), derive the handle shape from the node's Zod output object: each top-level key becomes its own readable sub-handle rooted at js/ctx.state.<key> (matching applyStepOutput Rule 2 Object.assign into state). Hard-error at authoring time if the node output is not a statically-known object (z.unknown/z.record/z.any) since spread cannot be made sound otherwise - mirror the existing load-time 'as and spread are mutually exclusive' guard style. No engine change: Rule 2 in PersistenceHelper.ts:76 is unchanged.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] spread:true on a node with output z.object({user,profile}) yields handles for state.user and state.profile, NOT state.<id>
- [ ] spread:true on a node with non-object output is a compile-time (or step()-call-time throw) error with an actionable message
- [ ] Reading state.<id> off a spread step is a compile error (the id slot does not exist under spread)

tasks.title: forEach per-item handle is closure-scoped and uncapturable
tasks.body:
forEach(handle, (item) => ...) must give a per-item handle whose TYPE is scoped to the callback so it cannot be referenced from any step after the forEach. Compile it to js/ctx.state.<as> to match ForEachNode.ts:586 (state[as]=item). Use a unique branded type minted inside the callback (and not assignable to the outer handle union) so capturing it in an outer-scope variable and reading it later is a compile error. Provide a typed itemIndex handle compiling to js/ctx.state.<as>Index. No ForEachNode runtime change.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] Inside the callback, item.field unwraps to js/ctx.state.<as>.field
- [ ] Referencing the per-item handle from a step declared after the forEach is a compile error

- [ ] Sequential and parallel ForEachNode runtime behaviour unchanged (existing ForEachNode tests still green)

**Edge cases to test:**

tasks.title: Named missing-state error for dangling $ref (loud failure)
tasks.body:
Add a thin, additive guard so a js/ctx.state.<id> reference that resolves to undefined-at-root produces a NamedMissingStateError naming the referenced step id AND the referencing step, instead of a generic 'Cannot read properties of undefined' that blames the mapper expression. This is the runtime backstop for R1/R2/R3/R7 - catches handle bugs that escape the type system (raw js/ strings, stale cross-runtime stubs, JSON twin authored by hand). Implement as a pre-check in the consuming step path or a refinement of MapperResolutionError.guessHint, NOT a rewrite of Mapper.runJs. Keep BLOK_MAPPER_MODE semantics (strict throws, warn logs, silent suppresses). Grounded in core/shared/src/utils/Mapper.ts guessHint:139 and handleResolutionError:388.

- [ ] silent mode unchanged
- [ ] Existing MapperResolutionError tests still pass (additive only)
- [ ] No change to the happy-path resolution cost (guard only fires on undefined-root)

Two-part adversarial test. Part A (R-replay, verify-only): prove a handle reference resolves identically on a fresh run and a cache-hit replay, since RunnerSteps.ts:551 routes the cache hit through applyStepOutput(ctx, step, {data: hit.data}) - the SAME persistence path. tasks.title: SPIKE: measure persist-all ctx.state growth + dispatch-payload blowout
tasks.body:
Quantify R5/R6 before committing the design at scale. Measure ctx.state memory for: (a) a workflow with 1k/5k/10k sequential persisted steps; (b) a forEach over 10k/50k items (confirm state[as] mutation stays O(1) not O(N) but the results[] array and node_runs persistence grow O(N)); (c) a spread-heavy pipeline. Then test the http-self sub-workflow path: a large ctx.state serialized as the child request body against BLOK_DISPATCH_PAYLOAD_MAX_BYTES (1MB) - confirm where the 413 PayloadTooLargeError surfaces and whether it is attributable. Deliver: a measured ceiling, a recommendation on whether an opt-in state-slot GC (drop slots no downstream handle references - computable from the handle graph at compile time) is needed for v1 or can be deferred, and whether ephemeral-by-default for side-effect nodes should be a lint.

**Acceptance:**

tasks.title: SPIKE: keep $ref structural in the IR vs pre-compile to js/ strings

Structural $ref lets the canvas, JSON twin, and AI statically detect dangling refs (ref to an undeclared/ephemeral/wrong-as step) and render edges WITHOUT executing - the single best place to catch R1/R2/R3 before runtime. Trade-off: a structural IR needs a compile pass at the runner boundary that the current js/-string path skips. Prototype the $ref-aware static validator over a few real workflows in triggers/http/src/workflows/.
