epics.title: EPIC: defineNode unchanged + import-registration (kill Nodes.ts)
epics.body:
The "defineNode() unchanged" half is true and safe: FunctionNode (core/runner/src/defineNode.ts) sets this.name = definition.name and is wired into the registry purely by the OUTSIDE — nothing in defineNode reads or derives a use-string, so it can stay byte-identical. The dangerous half is "use derived from node.name." It is FALSE against the live corpus. Today the registry key (what `use:` references and what moduleResolver looks up via opts.nodes.getNode(node.node)) is set by the HAND FILE triggers/http/src/Nodes.ts / HELPER_NODES / ExampleNodes maps — and that key diverges from node.name in three inconsistent patterns: (1) helper nodes set name === key (`name: "@blokjs/audit-log"`); (2) api-call/if-else set a bare name (`name: "api-call"`) while keyed `@blokjs/api-call`; (3) example nodes diverge arbitrarily (`key "base64-pdf"` vs `name "base64-to-pdf"`, `key "save-image"` vs `name "save-image-base64"`, `key "dashboard-ui"` vs `name "dashboard-generator-ui"`). So `use = node.name` would silently re-key the ENTIRE existing node library and break every workflow that says `use: "@blokjs/api-call"`. Worse, the keying is currently spread across THREE schemes: registry key (Nodes.ts), node.name (defineNode), and HMR relativePath (hmr/index.ts registers via nodeMap.addNode(event.relativePath, mod.default)). The JSON-path/Studio-palette concern is real and already half-broken: nodeCatalog.buildNodeCatalog emits `name: r.name ?? key` and THROWS THE KEY AWAY — so the palette already cannot tell an author the correct `use` string for api-call. "Auto-discovery" does NOT eliminate the hand-file unless it adopts ONE canonical key rule and the whole corpus is migrated to honor it. Verdict: the epic is buildable but the headline invariant must be reframed from "use = node.name" to "use = node.name AND every node is migrated so name IS the canonical fully-qualified ref" — which is a corpus migration, not a free derivation.

**Verdict:** `needs-rework`

**Risks / hard cases found in validation:**
- FALSE INVARIANT (highest): 'use derived from node.name' does not hold today. node.name != registry key for api-call (name 'api-call' / key '@blokjs/api-call'), if-else, and most example nodes (base64-pdf, save-image, dashboard-ui). Deriving use from name silently re-keys the whole library; every existing workflow referencing '@blokjs/api-call' gets 'Node ... not found'. Proven by reading nodes/web/api-call@1.0.0/index.ts:38, nodes/utility/helpers@1.0.0/src/index.ts, triggers/http/src/nodes/examples/index.ts.
- THREE competing keying schemes today: (a) registry key from the hand maps in triggers/http/src/Nodes.ts + HELPER_NODES + ExampleNodes; (b) node.name in defineNode; (c) HMR keys by FILE PATH (core/runner/src/hmr/index.ts: nodeMap.addNode(event.relativePath, mod.default)). 'import = registration' must reconcile all three or HMR re-registration will key a node differently from its boot-time key, so an edited node resolves under a stale key and the live one 404s.
- Collisions are SILENT today: Nodes.ts builds the map via object spread (...HELPER_NODES, ...EvalNodes, ...ExampleNodes). A user node whose name collides with a built-in (e.g. a user 'respond' or '@blokjs/log') overwrites the built-in last-wins with no warning. Killing the hand-file and auto-importing makes collisions MORE likely (no curated single map) while keeping them silent. There is no duplicate-name guard anywhere (grep found none).
- The JSON/palette path is already lossy: triggers/http/src/runner/nodeCatalog.ts:88 emits `name: r.name ?? key` and discards `key`. The Studio palette / JSON authoring therefore cannot surface the actual `use` string for any node where name != key. 'Auto-discovery for JSON workflows + palette' will reproduce the SAME hand-file by another name unless the canonical-key rule is enforced AND the catalog carries the resolvable ref, not the display name.
- Cross-runtime stub generation rests on schemas that are mostly NULL today: GrpcRuntimeAdapter.listNodes (core/runner/src/adapters/grpc/GrpcRuntimeAdapter.ts:474) returns inputSchema/outputSchema from parseSchemaBytes, and the code comment + parseSchemaBytes both confirm the SDKs largely don't emit schema bytes yet ('until then they're empty -> null'). A generated typed runtimeNode stub would have NO types for most runtime nodes — the typed-handle value proposition silently degrades to `unknown` exactly where authors most need types (cross-language boundary).
- type-inference via inferStepType (core/runner/src/workflow/WorkflowNormalizer.ts:1082) defaults EVERYTHING non-runtime-prefixed to 'module'. The 'local' type (NODES_PATH dynamic import, localResolver) is never inferred — it only fires on an explicit type. If import-registration is meant to replace the 'local' resolver path, that path's security canonicalization (Configuration.ts:769 'local node path escapes NODES_PATH') and its consumers must be audited; dropping it changes the threat model for user-supplied node refs.
- moduleResolver throws a hard 'Node X not found' on miss (Configuration.ts:629). Auto-discovery that misses a node (un-imported file, build artifact not present, tree-shaken import) converts a load-time-curated guarantee into a runtime 500 that only fires when that step executes — failures move later and get harder to attribute.
- Two nodes legitimately want the same SHORT name across packages (e.g. a 'query-generator' in two example bundles, or user + builtin). The fully-qualified '@scope/name' key disambiguates today; 'use = node.name' collapses the namespace and makes cross-package short-name collisions unresolvable without reintroducing a scope — i.e. reintroducing the very prefix that lives in the hand-file keys.

**Open questions to resolve:**
- What is the ONE canonical key rule? Options: (a) `name` must equal the fully-qualified ref and we migrate api-call/if-else/examples to `name: '@blokjs/api-call'` etc.; (b) keep `node.name` short and derive `use` from the package name + name; (c) keep an explicit, generated (not hand-written) manifest. The epic must pick one before any code — it changes every downstream task.
- On a name/use collision (user node shadows a built-in), is the desired behavior throw-at-startup, warn-and-last-wins (current implicit), or namespaced-coexist? This is a product decision with security implications (a user node shadowing '@blokjs/jwt-verify' is an auth bypass).
- Does 'import = registration' run at module-eval time (side-effectful import) or via an explicit barrel/glob the build generates? A glob-generated barrel IS a hand-file the build owns — acceptable, but it must be named as such so it isn't sold as 'no file'.
- For runtime nodes whose ListNodes returns null schemas, does `blokctl nodes sync` (a) generate an `unknown`-typed stub, (b) refuse to generate and require the SDK to emit schema, or (c) let the author hand-annotate? This gates whether cross-runtime typed handles are real or aspirational.
- How does HMR re-key on edit so the edited node replaces the SAME registry entry it had at boot? Today it keys by relativePath; the canonical rule must be applied identically in hmr/index.ts and at boot, or edits silently fork the registry.

Produce a table and pick: (a) migrate all names to fully-qualified refs, or (b) derive use = packageName + '/' + name, or (c) build-generated manifest barrel. With auto-import there is no curated order, so collisions are MORE likely and currently silent — a user node named like a built-in (e.g. '@blokjs/log', 'respond') would shadow it. Make NodeMap.addNode (or the registration wrapper) detect a re-add of an existing key and either throw or warn-loudly per the SPIKE decision, with the two source locations in the message. Do NOT change addNode's signature used by HMR re-registration (re-adds during HMR are legitimate and must be allowed — distinguish boot collision from HMR replace).

epics.title: Typed runtimeNode stubs + ListNodes manifest codegen
epics.body:
The plumbing this epic depends on is ALREADY BUILT and verified in all 7 SDKs: every SDK implements gRPC ListNodes returning NodeDescriptor{input_schema_json, output_schema_json} (Go sdks/go/grpc_server.go:207, Python sdks/python3/blok/server/grpc_server.py:275, Ruby grpc_server.rb:80, PHP BlokNodeRuntimeService.php:87, plus Rust/Java/C#). The runner already aggregates them: core/runner GrpcRuntimeAdapter.listNodes() parses the schema bytes (line 474), triggers/http/src/runner/nodeCatalog.ts:buildNodeCatalog() flattens module + all runtime nodes into GET /__blok/nodes, and blokctl nodes list (packages/cli/src/commands/nodes/listNodes.ts) already consumes it. The ONLY new work is: turn that catalog into typed TS `runtimeNode` stub modules (codegen), and wire `blokctl nodes sync` + dev-loop regen. So this epic is mostly a thin codegen layer over proven infra — DON'T re-implement ListNodes. The hard part is NOT 'do the SDKs report schema' (they do) — it's what happens when they report NOTHING. Schema is OPTIONAL in every single SDK: untyped/legacy handlers return nil (Go define_node.go:79 reflectSchemaJSON returns nil on error; Python input_json_schema returns None when no Pydantic model; Ruby returns nil output_schema_json; PHP inputSchema():?array). The dynamic langs reflect from a DECLARED model (Pydantic / Ruby field-DSL / PHP array), NOT a raw fs-scan of arbitrary function bodies — so an undeclared dynamic node is a typeless black box. A stub generated for a no-schema node is the central edge case and the whole correctness story rides on it.

**Verdict:** `sound-with-risks`

**Risks / hard cases found in validation:**
- NO-SCHEMA NODES ARE THE COMMON CASE, NOT THE EDGE. Every SDK returns nil/None when a node lacks a typed model (Go define_node.go:79, Python define_node.py:124 returns None, Ruby typed_node.rb:132 nil out-schema, PHP NodeReflector ?array). Dynamic langs (Python/Ruby/PHP) reflect from a DECLARED Pydantic model / field-DSL / array — NOT an fs-scan of arbitrary code as the research brief's 'fs-scan' framing implies. An author who writes a plain Python dict-handler or legacy Go map-handler gets ZERO schema. The stub for such a node MUST degrade to an untyped handle (inputs: Record<string,unknown>, output: unknown) — NOT crash codegen, NOT emit `any` silently as if typed. If the stub claims a type it doesn't have, the new typed-handle DSL gives FALSE confidence and the soundness claim of the whole vision collapses for sidecar nodes.
- CHICKEN-AND-EGG: listNodes() is a LIVE gRPC call (GrpcRuntimeAdapter.ts:474) — it requires the sidecar process running. So `nodes sync` cannot generate stubs for a runtime that won't boot. On a clean checkout / CI / fresh clone with no runtimes started, sync produces nothing. 'Codegen on blokctl dev' has a race: dev spawns the sidecars AND the runner; stubs must be generated AFTER each sidecar's Health flips SERVING, or sync hits a connection-refused and silently emits empty/stale stubs. The existing buildNodeCatalog SWALLOWS unreachable-runtime errors (nodeCatalog.ts:113 `catch {}`) — perfect for a live catalog view, FATAL for codegen: a flaky/slow runtime would silently drop all its stubs and break every workflow importing them with no error.
- STALE STUBS vs PROTO/SCHEMA DRIFT: stubs are generated artifacts checked in (like gen app-types' blok-app.d.ts). If an author edits a Go node's struct and forgets `nodes sync`, the .ts stub's type lies — typecheck passes against the OLD shape, runtime fails when the mapper resolves a $ref to a field the new node never returns. Nothing detects this unless sync runs in CI with a git-dirty check. Proto drift compounds it: the research brief flags SDK protos can go stale vs canonical (risk #1 in understand-runtimes.md); a stale proto means input_schema_json field decodes wrong/empty.
- SAME NODE NAME ACROSS RUNTIMES is explicitly allowed (understand-runtimes.md invariant #3: name unique PER runtime, not globally). buildNodeCatalog flattens `validate-card`@runtime.go AND `validate-card`@runtime.python3 into one list differentiated only by `runtime` field. The vision says `use` is DERIVED FROM node.name — but two runtimes both expose `validate-card`, so the derived `use:'validate-card'` is AMBIGUOUS and runtimeResolver (Configuration.ts:492) defaults to python3 when no runtime specified. Stub codegen must namespace by runtime (e.g. one module per runtime.kind, or use:'runtime.go/validate-card') or workflows silently route to the wrong language.
- JSON-SCHEMA → TS TYPE FIDELITY: no json-schema-to-typescript dep exists in the repo (checked package.json / cli / runner). Pydantic, go-jsonschema (Reflector{DoNotReference,ExpandedStruct}), Ruby's hand-rolled {type:object,properties}, and PHP arrays emit DIFFERENT JSON-Schema dialects (draft versions, $ref expansion, format keywords, nullable vs anyOf[null], additionalProperties defaults, enum/oneOf, recursive types). A converter that's correct for Pydantic's draft-2020 output can silently mistype Ruby's bespoke schema. The typed handle reads x.field — if the converter drops a property or widens it to unknown, the structural $ref still resolves at runtime but the author loses the type guarantee the vision is sold on.
- THE 'fs-scan dynamic langs' PREMISE IN THE EPIC IS PARTLY WRONG and worth correcting: Python/Ruby/PHP do NOT fs-scan to PRODUCE a schema — they fs-scan to DISCOVER node files (BLOK_NODES_DIR), then reflect the schema from a declared model inside each file. No declared model ⇒ no schema, regardless of fs-scan. So 'do dynamic langs have a schema to report' = ONLY IF the author declared one. This is a real gap for the marketing claim 'all 7 runtimes get typed stubs'.

**Open questions to resolve:**
- When a node reports no schema, does the stub emit (a) an untyped handle Record<string,unknown>→unknown, (b) skip the node entirely and warn (like gen app-types skips JSON workflows), or (c) fail sync? Recommend (a) so the node is still callable, with a // no-schema comment + a warning listing untyped nodes — never silent `any`.
- Where do stubs live and are they git-checked-in (like blok-app.d.ts) or gitignored generated artifacts? Checked-in enables CI drift detection; gitignored avoids merge noise but needs a build-step guarantee.
- Should `nodes sync` read the live GET /__blok/nodes (reuse buildNodeCatalog, needs server up) or call each adapter.listNodes() directly (needs sidecars up but not the full trigger)? Reusing the HTTP endpoint is the laziest correct path and already battle-tested.
- How is same-name-across-runtimes disambiguated in the authoring DSL — per-runtime module namespacing (import {validateCard} from './nodes/go') or a qualified use string? This blocks the codegen output shape.
- Does the dev-loop regen on sidecar Health=SERVING (event-driven) or poll? And does a sidecar that never boots block dev startup or just warn + skip its stubs?
- Should sync FAIL loudly (non-zero exit) when a declared runtime is unreachable, vs the live-catalog's swallow-and-continue? Codegen needs the strict variant — partial stubs that drop a runtime's nodes are worse than no run.

tasks.title: Spike: capture real ListNodes JSON-Schema from all 7 SDKs and diff the dialects
tasks.body:
Boot each sidecar (or run its ListNodes test fixture) and dump the actual input_schema_json/output_schema_json bytes for a typed node AND an untyped node. Go uses invopop/santhosh-tekuri jsonschema Reflector{DoNotReference,ExpandedStruct} (define_node.go:79); Python uses Pydantic model_json_schema() (draft 2020-12); Ruby hand-rolls {type:object,properties,required} (typed_node.rb:107); PHP returns a raw array; Rust/Java/C# TBD. Catalog the concrete differences: draft version, $ref/$defs presence, nullable encoding (anyOf[null] vs nullable:true), format keywords, additionalProperties default, enum vs oneOf, recursive/self-referential types. Output: a fixtures dir of real schemas + a table of which JSON-Schema features each SDK emits. This DECIDES whether an off-the-shelf json-schema-to-typescript handles all 7 or needs per-dialect normalization.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] Real captured schema JSON committed as fixtures for >=1 typed + 1 untyped node per available SDK
- [ ] Documented table of dialect differences across the 7 SDKs

**Edge cases to test:**

Unlike the live `nodes list`, sync must be STRICT about unreachable runtimes: buildNodeCatalog swallows adapter.listNodes() rejections (nodeCatalog.ts:113) — for codegen, detect a declared-but-missing runtime (cross-check .blok/config.json runtimes vs what the catalog returned) and FAIL with a clear message rather than silently dropping that runtime's stubs. Group results by runtime kind. ponytail: reuse the HTTP endpoint + NodeEntry shape rather than opening fresh gRPC channels.

tasks.title: Generate typed runtimeNode stub modules from catalog (incl. no-schema degradation)
tasks.body:
Convert each catalog entry's input/output JSON Schema to a TS type and emit a `runtimeNode` stub (the node ref + typed input/output) the DSL's step() consumes to shape its typed handle. Mirror gen app-types (appTypes.ts): static emit, no project-code execution, warn-and-list gaps. CRITICAL no-schema path: when inputSchema/outputSchema is null (the COMMON case — every SDK returns nil for untyped nodes), emit an untyped stub (input: Record<string,unknown>, output: unknown) with a `// no schema reported by runtime.<kind>` comment, and collect the node into a warning list printed at the end (exactly like app-types listing skipped JSON workflows). NEVER silently emit `any` as if typed. Namespace stubs per runtime kind so same-name-across-runtimes (validate-card on go AND python3) don't collide — one module/dir per runtime.<kind>. Use the json-schema converter chosen by SPIKE-schema-fidelity.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] Typed node → stub with a precise TS input/output type matching the reported schema
- [ ] No-schema node → untyped stub (Record<string,unknown>/unknown) + appears in end-of-run warning list, never silent any
- [ ] Same node name in two runtimes produces two distinct, non-colliding stubs namespaced by runtime kind
- [ ] Generated file header marks it generated + names the source command (re-run hint), like blok-app.d.ts

**Edge cases to test:**
- Node name not a valid TS identifier (kebab-case, leading digit, dot) — sanitize like nameToIdentifier (appTypes.ts:58)
- Schema with additionalProperties open → index signature, not a closed type
- Empty {} schema (nodeCatalog normSchema:48 already maps {} → null) → treated as no-schema
- Output schema present but input null (or vice-versa) — partial typing must still emit
- A schema dialect the converter can't handle → fall back to untyped + warn, never throw mid-file

A sidecar that never boots must WARN + skip its stubs WITHOUT blocking dev or other runtimes' stubs. On a runtime crash+restart mid-session, regen on recovery (its node set may have changed). ponytail: a single debounced regen triggered when the set of SERVING runtimes changes, not a per-node watcher.

epics.title: @blokjs/core consolidation + packaging
epics.body:
The merge of runner+shared+helper into one @blokjs/core is structurally SOUND on the dependency graph: shared has no internal deps, helper depends only on zod (NOT shared — confirmed, no grep hit), and runner depends on both helper and shared. That is a clean DAG, so there is no circular-dependency risk between the three merged units, and lockstep release (release.ts PUBLISHABLE list + checkLockstepVersion + checkCrossDepRanges + checkCliConstants) can be retired for these three.

BUT the tree-shaking and back-compat claims do NOT survive scrutiny as written:

1. NO package in the repo declares `sideEffects: false` (verified across core/*, triggers/*, nodes/**). The runner barrel (core/runner/src/index.ts, 482 lines) re-exports grpc, otel, and sqlite-store symbols as VALUE exports. A single `@blokjs/core` entry that exposes both the DSL (`workflow`, `$`) and the runner means `import { workflow } from "@blokjs/core"` drags the runner's transitive grpc/otel/better-sqlite3 surface into the workflow-authoring bundle unless the exports map splits subpaths (`@blokjs/core/dsl` vs `@blokjs/core/runtime`). "Unused triggers/nodes drop" is independently fine (triggers/nodes stay separate packages), but "the DSL stays light" requires an exports-map split that the spec does not name.

2. The "PersistenceHelper + Mapper are byte-identical" claim is HALF TRUE. PersistenceHelper.applyStepOutput is genuinely unchanged. But `{ $ref: { step, path } }` does NOT exist in the codebase today — `$` currently compiles to `js/ctx.<path>` STRINGS via unwrapProxies (JS_EXPR_TAG deep-walk), and Mapper resolves them by stripping `js/` (slice(3)) and eval-ing against ctx. Structural $ref is NEW wire format; either the mapper gains a new $ref branch (NOT byte-identical) or the DSL must LOWER handles to `js/ctx.state.<step>.<path>` strings before the runner ever sees them (then the engine is unchanged but the "structural IR" claim weakens). This fork must be decided before building.

3. Back-compat blast radius is large: 144 files import @blokjs/runner, 137 import @blokjs/shared, 99 import @blokjs/helper (deduped file counts). The deprecated re-export shims must cover the FULL public surface of all three, including value exports (createStore, defineNode, RuntimeRegistry) and type-only exports — a wildcard `export * from "@blokjs/core"` shim can subtly break `import type` elision and dual type/value names.

Verdict: sound-with-risks. The packaging merge is safe; the authoring-surface ($ref) and tree-shaking claims need a spike + tests before the founder calls it bulletproof.

**Verdict:** `sound-with-risks`

**Risks / hard cases found in validation:**
- TREE-SHAKING UNVERIFIED: no `sideEffects:false` exists anywhere. Merging the runner barrel (re-exports grpc/otel/sqlite as VALUE exports) with the DSL into one entry means `import {workflow}` pulls the runtime's heavy transitive graph. Needs an exports-map subpath split (@blokjs/core/dsl vs runtime) + sideEffects:false + a bundle-size assertion, or the 'unused drops' claim is false.
- $ref IS NEW CODE, NOT THE EXISTING ENGINE: today `$` lowers to `js/ctx....` strings (unwrapProxies + JS_EXPR_TAG) and Mapper resolves via slice(3) eval. `{$ref:{step,path}}` appears nowhere in core/. The 'mapper is byte-identical' claim is FALSE unless handles lower to js/ strings at definition time. Decide: new mapper $ref branch vs lower-to-js/. Each has different soundness/perf.
- SHIM SURFACE IS HUGE + MIXED: 144/137/99 files import runner/shared/helper. Shims must re-export value AND type exports verbatim. `export *` risks dual-name (type+value same identifier like FunctionNode/FnNodeDefinition) collisions and breaks `import type` elision. A deprecation console.warn at import time would fire on EVERY boot for unmigrated users — annoying and un-silenceable without an env flag.
- AUTO-DISCOVERY AMBIGUITY: Nodes.ts mixes package-named nodes (`@blokjs/api-call` where package name == node name) with local nodes (`chain-init`, name-derived) and a SPREAD map (`...HELPER_NODES` = 20+ nodes from ONE package). 'import = registration, use derived from node.name' breaks for the helpers package: one import yields many node.names. Auto-discovery must handle 1-package-N-nodes and name collisions (two packages both export node.name 'log').
- RELEASE.TS PARTIAL TEARDOWN: checkCrossDepRanges/checkLockstepVersion span ALL 16 packages, not just the 3 merging. Removing lockstep for core while triggers+nodes+cli STILL lockstep (scaffold needs them co-versioned, see the v0.6 comment block) means release.ts becomes a HYBRID: independent @blokjs/core + still-lockstepped rest. checkCliConstants (GITHUB_REPO_RELEASE_TAG/BLOKJS_DEP_RANGE pin) assumes one version — it breaks the moment core floats independently.
- RUNTIME-STUB MANIFEST DRIFT: runtimeNode stub generated from ListNodes JSON-Schema manifest (proto rpc confirmed at runtime.proto:46). If a Go node changes its output schema and `blokctl nodes sync` isn't rerun, the typed handle in TS lies about the shape — silent type/runtime divergence with no compile error. The sync step is an out-of-band manual gate with no staleness check.
- IR/JSON-TWIN DOES NOT EXIST YET: grep for toIR/WorkflowIR/jsonTwin/emitIR returns nothing. The '.ts compiles to language-neutral JSON IR consumed by canvas/AI' is greenfield, not a refactor. forEach/branch/tryCatch currently emit V2*Step shapes (StepOpts.ts), not a normalized {use,type,inputs:{$ref}} IR. The canvas+JSON-twin+AI consumers are all unbuilt and unvalidated against round-trip fidelity.

**Open questions to resolve:**
- $ref lowering: does the DSL emit structural `{$ref}` (new mapper branch) or lower to `js/ctx.state.<step>.<path>` strings at definition time (engine unchanged)? This is the single highest-leverage decision and gates whether 'byte-identical engine' is true.
- Exports-map shape for @blokjs/core: one entry (`.`) or split subpaths (`./dsl`, `./runtime`, `./testing`)? The DSL-in-author-bundle weight depends entirely on this.
- Lockstep: does ending lockstep apply ONLY to @blokjs/core, or also to triggers and nodes? release.ts's scaffold-pin logic (trigger-http imports 4 sibling packages by ^version) assumes co-versioning — partial decoupling needs a documented version-compat matrix.
- Deprecation shim: console.warn on import (noisy, every boot) vs JSDoc @deprecated only (silent, IDE-only) vs a one-time process-level warn? And for how many minor versions do shims live before removal?
- Node-name collision policy when two independently-published packages both export a node whose `node.name` is the same string — last-import-wins (current Map behavior) or hard error at discovery?
- Does `blokctl nodes sync` get a staleness/hash check so a stale runtimeNode stub fails CI rather than silently lying about a cross-runtime node's output shape?

tasks.body:

Today `$` compiles to `js/ctx.<path>` strings (core/workflow-helper/src/proxy/$.ts, unwrapProxies + JS_EXPR_TAG) and core/shared/src/utils/Mapper.ts resolves them by slice(3)+eval. The proposed `step()` handle records `{$ref:{step,path}}` which does NOT exist. Prototype BOTH: (a) DSL lowers handles to `js/ctx.state.<step>.<path>` strings at definition time (Mapper untouched — proves the byte-identical claim); (b) Mapper gains a `{$ref}` resolution branch alongside js/. Compare: type-safety of the handle, round-trip to JSON IR, perf (eval vs structural lookup), and what breaks tryCatch's `ctx.state[id]===undefined` truthiness check. Output a recommendation + the exact resolution path.

tasks.title: Auto-discovery to replace Nodes.ts: import=registration, handle 1-package-N-nodes + collisions
tasks.body:
Delete the hand-maintained Nodes.ts (triggers/http/src/Nodes.ts and siblings) in favor of auto-discovery. The hard cases the spec glosses: (1) `use` derived from node.name works for `chain-init` (local, name='chain-init') but the helpers package exports HELPER_NODES — ONE import yielding 20+ node.names, so 'import = one node' is false; discovery must accept a default export that is a node OR a map of nodes. (2) Two packages exporting the same node.name (e.g. 'log') collide in the flat Map — define a policy (hard error at discovery with both source packages named, NOT last-wins which is the current silent Map behavior). (3) Cross-runtime nodes get a generated runtimeNode stub from the ListNodes manifest (proto rpc exists, runtime.proto:46) — discovery registers stubs whose `use` comes from the manifest, not a TS import. JSON workflows + Studio palette resolve via the same discovery, not the hand file.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] Nodes.ts deleted; local TS nodes register by import with use=node.name

- [ ] Cross-runtime nodes register from the ListNodes manifest via blokctl nodes sync
- [ ] JSON workflows and Studio /__blok/nodes resolve through discovery, not a hand file

Prove the JSON IR is the artifact the npm-like registry/marketplace publishes and installs: define the admission gate (validateWorkflow as a real gate at publish, not just advisory), prove a published IR round-trips back to a runnable workflow on install, and define how a workflow's runtimeNode stub dependencies (cross-runtime nodes) are declared in and resolved from the published artifact. epics.title: Codemods + back-compat + hybrid coexistence
epics.body:
Validated the codemod/back-compat/hybrid story against the real tree (1611 TS + 545 JSON workflows; the real $ proxy compiler at core/workflow-helper/src/proxy/$.ts; eq.ts comparators; triggers/http/src/Nodes.ts; WorkflowNormalizer). Five load-bearing holes the deck glosses over. (1) Un-migratable expressions are the COMMON case: 58 of 338 js/ expressions in shipped JSON contain real logic (|| defaults, ?? , ternaries, [...spread,{...}], process.env, Date.now(), Array.isArray(x)?x:[], (x||'').toLowerCase()) — none map to a handle or tpl. On the most-copied workflows (agent, chat, webhook-github, stripe) the codemod's PRIMARY output is hand-migrate markers, not migrated code. (2) One regex cannot own js/: the same string surface feeds step inputs (Mapper→handle-eligible), @blokjs/expr's expression (raw IIFE JS, must NOT be touched — double-eval), idempotency/concurrency/debounce key (resolveIdempotencyKey custom eval, fail-OPEN, no handle form), forEach.in/switch.on iterables, and polymorphic subworkflow dispatch. A blanket rewrite corrupts the last four. (3) use: is not a unique node key — cross-runtime-chain.json reuses use:chain-test across 5 runtime.* kinds, so stub generation keyed on use alone collides; the (use,type) PAIR is the key. (4) The new branch(handle,{then:()=>..}) closure API is structurally different from today's branch({when,then:[...]}) arrays — steps register by call-order side-effect inside a closure, so the IR-production path for control flow is NEW, not byte-identical; branch.ts today calls unwrapProxies(opts.then) on an array. The engine-identity claim holds only for the Mapper/PersistenceHelper on the RESOLVED IR. (5) The comparator/branch.when codemod is lossy: today's eq() canonicalizes aliases (ctx.req→ctx.request) and compound whens (a && b, .toLowerCase()) have no eq/gt/lt form, so most real whens stay raw strings too. Verdict: the engine-reuse half is sound; the codemod half is sound-with-significant-risks and needs a hard schemaVersion gate, a no-op-by-default coverage harness, and honest marking of the un-migratable majority.

**Verdict:** `sound-with-risks`

**Risks / hard cases found in validation:**
- UN-MIGRATABLE IS THE MAJORITY ON HOT WORKFLOWS: 58/338 js/ expressions carry logic; tpl only covers pure paths; the codemod must emit markers for them and report coverage honestly or the migration looks broken.
- js/ IS OVERLOADED ACROSS 4+ RESOLVERS: step inputs (handle-eligible) vs @blokjs/expr expression (raw IIFE, must not touch) vs idempotency/concurrency/debounce key (fail-open custom eval, no handle form) vs forEach.in/switch.on vs polymorphic subworkflow. A blanket rewrite corrupts the last four; codemod must be field-aware.
- use: IS NOT UNIQUE: cross-runtime-chain.json reuses use:chain-test across 5 runtime kinds; stub/import resolution must key on the (use,type) pair, and runtime.* nodes are not even in Nodes.ts.
- BRANCH/forEach IR-PRODUCTION IS NEW: closure form collects steps by side-effect, structurally unlike today's unwrapProxies(array); the byte-identical claim holds only for the Mapper/PersistenceHelper on the resolved IR, not the IR-building path.
- COMPOUND branch.when HAS NO TYPED-OP TARGET (&&, .toLowerCase(), typeof, !=null) and the new branch(boolean-handle) can't express them, so hybrid coexistence is permanent, not transitional.
- ctx.prev/$.prev→handle is unsafe after ephemeral or non-adjacent steps (prev is overwritten every step); only literal adjacent non-ephemeral is convertible.
- JSON workflows + TS→JSON round-trips lose Zod schemas, so migrated handles there are untyped any — the compile-safety headline doesn't apply and must be qualified.
- No explicit schemaVersion exists; partially-migrated mixed-mode files are undetectable without a gate and can silently mis-run.
- blokctl install node regex-patches Nodes.ts in place; deleting the file breaks that CLI path and any user tooling that greps/patches it.

**Open questions to resolve:**
- Is there an explicit schemaVersion/dsl marker on the envelope, or must the hybrid runner keep inferring from structure? An explicit gate is near-mandatory for mixed-mode detection.
- For fail-open keys (idempotency/concurrency/debounce), does the new model keep raw expression strings (no handle)? If so, 'no js/ anywhere in authoring' is false and must be qualified.
- Does step() register via ambient builder side-effect or closure return? Determines testability and whether conditional step() corrupts the IR (see SPIKE-CLOSURE-IR).
- When a node name exists in multiple runtimes, what is the stub filename/import-identity convention, and does step() carry the runtime kind so (use,type) survives into the IR?
- Must the two codemods be idempotent and order-independent (refs-first vs Nodes-first)? Golden files must pin this.
- What auto-migration rate counts as success given 17% logic-bearing refs concentrated in flagship examples?

tasks.title: Field-aware ref codemod: $.state/js/path → handle reads + tpl, scoped to handle-eligible positions ONLY
tasks.body:
Build Codemod 1 over step inputs ONLY. Use an AST walk (ts-morph for .ts, JSON-path for JSON), not a global regex. Allowlist the positions it may rewrite: step.inputs.* values. EXPLICITLY EXCLUDE: @blokjs/expr expression inputs, idempotencyKey/concurrencyKey/debounce.key, forEach.in, switch.on, subworkflow dispatch expressions, branch.when. For an eligible value: a pure path ($.state.<id>.a.b or js/ctx.state.<id>.a.b) → handle read <id>.a.b; a pure-path template (js/`...${ctx.state.x}...`) → tpl`...${x}...`. Anything containing an operator/call/spread/ternary/optional-chain/process.env/Date/Array. is NOT a pure path → leave verbatim and emit a `// blok-migrate: hand-migrate (dynamic expression)` marker above the step. Map ctx.req/$.req→req handle; ctx.prev/$.prev→adjacent handle ONLY if previous step is non-ephemeral and structurally adjacent, else mark.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] Pure-path inputs ($.state.x.y, js/ctx.state.x.y) rewrite to handle reads; pure-path templates rewrite to tpl
- [ ] Any value containing || ?? ?. ternary, [...spread], object literal, process.env, Date., Array., (..)(), .toLowerCase() etc. is left verbatim with a hand-migrate marker
- [ ] expression:/key fields/forEach.in/switch.on/subworkflow/branch.when are NEVER rewritten by this codemod
- [ ] ctx.prev/$.prev only rewrites to a handle when the previous step is non-ephemeral and structurally adjacent; otherwise marked
- [ ] Codemod is idempotent: re-running on its own output is a no-op (already-handle code and marked code both skipped)

**Edge cases to test:**
- js/`agent:${ctx.request.params.sessionId}:history` in a key field must be untouched
- (ctx.state.attempt ?? 0) + 1 must be marked, not partially rewritten
- $.state['load-history'] bracket/quoted id must resolve to the same handle as the step id 'load-history'
- spread:true step's downstream refs read top-level state keys (state.user) not state.<id>.user — handle must mirror the spread
- ctx.prev after an ephemeral step must be marked (no state slot to point a handle at)

*estimate XL*

tasks.title: Golden-file corpus for Codemod 1 including the un-migratable majority
tasks.body:
Assemble a golden corpus from the REAL shipped workflows that exercise every hard case found: agent-message.json / chat-message.json (spread+ternary+process.env), webhook-github*.json ((x||'').toLowerCase(), || null), v05-* (compound whens), stripe (deep optional-chain ??), countries-vs-facts.json (raw when), plus spread/as/ephemeral cases. For each input, pin a golden output. Assert: (a) every logic-bearing js/ becomes a hand-migrate marker, never a guessed handle; (b) every pure path/template migrates; (c) expression/key/forEach.in/subworkflow fields are byte-identical pre/post; (d) idempotency: codemod(codemod(x)) === codemod(x); (e) a COVERAGE REPORT counts migrated vs marked per file and the suite asserts the marked set matches expectation exactly (no silent over- or under-migration).

**Acceptance:**

- [ ] Test asserts expression:/key/forEach.in/subworkflow bytes unchanged

tasks.title: Nodes.ts removal codemod: (use,type) pair → direct import or runtimeNode stub; CLI install path redirect
tasks.body:
Codemod 2 resolves each step's (use, type) PAIR — not use alone — against the existing Nodes.ts map + runtime adapters. type:'module'/absent + use in map → add a direct import of the mapped node module and replace use:string with the imported binding in step(). type:'runtime.<kind>' → reference a generated runtimeNode stub (produced by NODE-SYNC), keyed and filenamed by (name,kind) so chain-test across 5 runtimes yields 5 distinct stubs/imports. Unresolvable use (not in map, no runtime) → mark, do not guess. Delete Nodes.ts only after all referencing workflows in the project migrate. Add a deprecation+redirect for `blokctl install node` which today regex-patches Nodes.ts: it must instead no-op-with-warning or scaffold a direct-import hint, never silently fail.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] Resolution keys on (use,type) pair; runtime.* nodes resolve to stubs, not the in-process map
- [ ] Same node name across multiple runtimes produces distinct stub files + distinct imports (no collision/overwrite)
- [ ] Unresolvable use is marked, never guessed into an import
- [ ] Nodes.ts deleted only when zero workflows still reference it via string
- [ ] blokctl install node no longer silently regex-patches a deleted file — it warns/redirects

tasks.title: Hybrid normalizer: handle-DSL, v2-array, and v1 all normalize to one resolved IR; reject mixed-mode files
tasks.body:
Extend WorkflowNormalizer so the new handle/closure DSL produces the SAME canonical InternalWorkflow shape (steps[] + nodes{} map) that v1/v2 already produce, so the Mapper + PersistenceHelper + Configuration consume it unchanged. Add an explicit schemaVersion (or dsl marker) on the envelope and gate detection on it rather than pure structure. The handle DSL's branch(handle,{then:()=>..}) and forEach(handle,(item)=>..) closures must be evaluated at definition time into the same arm-step arrays + flat nodes{} entries the array form yields, preserving assertNoDuplicateStepIds across arms, ephemeral/as/spread, and step order. A file that is partially migrated (mix of handle steps and leftover array/string steps) must be DETECTED and rejected with a clear error, not silently mis-run.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] Handle DSL, v2 array, and v1 all yield byte-identical resolved IR for an equivalent workflow
- [ ] Explicit schemaVersion/dsl gate drives detection; structural inference is the fallback only
- [ ] Closure-form branch/forEach produce the same nodes{} + steps[] as the array form, with id-uniqueness enforced across arms
- [ ] ephemeral/as/spread options-bag on step() map to the same persistence flags as the array DSL
- [ ] A partially-migrated mixed-mode file is rejected with an actionable error

**Edge cases to test:**
- A user-level if/loop wrapping a step() call inside a branch closure (conditional registration) — must error or be deterministic, not produce a phantom/missing step
- Nested branch-in-forEach-in-tryCatch closures: arm step collection order and id-scoping
- as: used to let two branch arms write the same downstream key (id:runA as:run / id:runB as:run) must still pass the duplicate-id guard

*estimate XL*

tasks.title: Equivalence + mixed-mode-rejection test: three DSLs, one IR, identical run trace
tasks.body:
For a representative workflow (multi-step + branch + forEach + ephemeral + spread + as), author it three ways: v1 array, v2 array, new handle DSL. Assert all three normalize to a deep-equal resolved IR, and run all three through WorkflowTestRunner asserting identical step traces, identical state[id] contents, and identical final response (the 'byte-identical engine' claim, tested end to end). Add cases asserting partially-migrated mixed files are rejected, conditional step() registration inside a closure is handled deterministically, and cross-arm id collisions still throw at load.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] Deep-equal IR across v1/v2/handle for the same workflow
- [ ] Identical run trace + state + response across all three via WorkflowTestRunner
- [ ] Mixed-mode file rejection asserted
- [ ] Cross-arm duplicate-id + as-aliasing behavior asserted identical to array DSL
- [ ] Conditional step() inside a closure produces a deterministic, asserted outcome (error or stable IR)

**Edge cases to test:**
- ephemeral step in the middle breaks ctx.prev-equivalence — handle form must still resolve the far-upstream read
- spread step: downstream handle reads top-level keys in all three forms
- forEach per-item handle vs v1 ctx.state[as] + [as+'Index'] parity

*estimate L · depends: HYBRID-RUNNER, CM-1*

tasks.title: blokctl nodes sync: emit runtimeNode<In,Out> stubs from ListNodes manifest, keyed by (name,runtime)
tasks.body:
Implement the codegen that, for each of the 7 runtimes, reads the existing ListNodes RPC (NodeDescriptor.input_schema_json/output_schema_json) and writes a typed runtimeNode stub. Key stub identity and filename on (name, runtime-kind) so duplicate names across runtimes don't collide. JSON-Schema→TS type conversion must handle the real schema shapes Blok nodes emit (z.union outputs like api-call's string|record, optional/default fields, z.unknown, z.record). When a manifest field can't be represented (truly dynamic z.unknown), emit `unknown` (typed escape), never `any`, and never fabricate fields. Gate the whole command behind reachability checks (runtime container health) and fail loudly if a runtime is down rather than emitting stale/empty stubs.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] One stub per (name,runtime); same name across runtimes → distinct files/idents
- [ ] z.union / optional / default / z.record / z.unknown outputs convert to faithful TS (unknown for truly-dynamic, never any)
- [ ] Stubs compile and the emitted (use,type) IR matches what the runner resolves today
- [ ] Runtime-down → loud failure, not silent empty/stale stub
- [ ] Re-running sync is deterministic (stable output for unchanged manifest)

**Edge cases to test:**
- api-call's z.union([z.string(), z.record(z.unknown())]) output — handle must be usable but its field access is unprovable; surface as union, not any
- A node whose output schema is just z.unknown() — handle is unknown, reads require a cast (document this limitation)
- Manifest schema drift between sync and run — stub is stale; need a freshness/version check

*estimate L*

tasks.title: Deprecated re-export shims for @blokjs/runner / shared / helper / helpers; end-lockstep packaging
tasks.body:
Make @blokjs/core re-export the public surface of the old packages and publish @blokjs/runner, @blokjs/shared, @blokjs/helper, @blokjs/helpers as thin deprecated shims that re-export from core (with a console deprecation notice once per process). Verify the one-letter helper/helpers trap is preserved for back-compat (both names still resolve to the right thing). Confirm existing node packages that declare @blokjs/runner as a peer/dep keep resolving without a duplicate-install (single core instance). This is the lever that lets old projects build untouched while new ones import only @blokjs/core.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] Old imports (@blokjs/runner, @blokjs/helper, @blokjs/helpers, @blokjs/shared) resolve via shims
- [ ] @blokjs/helper (DSL) vs @blokjs/helpers (nodes bag) both still resolve correctly
- [ ] No duplicate-install: defineNode identity and instanceof checks hold across core and shims
- [ ] Deprecation notice emitted once, not per-call
- [ ] An existing unmodified project builds against the shimmed packages

**Edge cases to test:**
- A node package pinning @blokjs/runner@^0.7 alongside @blokjs/core@^1 — version-skew duplicate runtime instance
- Symbol-based identity (JS_EXPR_TAG, branded envelopes) must be the SAME symbol across core and shim, or proxy/respond detection breaks
- Tree-shaking a shim re-export

*estimate M · depends: HYBRID-RUNNER*

tasks.title: Shim identity + version-skew test
tasks.body:
Assert that branded-envelope detection (workflow _blokV2, @blokjs/respond envelope, JS_EXPR_TAG proxy detection) works when the producer imports from a shim and the consumer imports from core (and vice versa) — the symbols/brands must be identical instances. Add a version-skew scenario (node dep on old runner shim + app on core) and assert no double-instance breakage in the Mapper/PersistenceHelper path.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] Cross-import branded detection passes both directions
- [ ] JS_EXPR_TAG proxy from shim is unwrapped by core's unwrapProxies
- [ ] Version-skew scenario does not produce two runtime instances that fail instanceof/symbol checks

**Edge cases to test:**
- respond envelope created via shim, unpacked by core HttpTrigger
- $ proxy from @blokjs/helper shim used in a @blokjs/core workflow()

*estimate S · depends: BACKCOMPAT-SHIMS*

tasks.title: branch.when codemod: trivial single-comparison → eq/gt/lt or boolean handle; everything else marked + stays raw
tasks.body:
Convert ONLY the safely-convertible subset of branch.when: a single binary comparison of a pure path against a literal (ctx.state.x.inStock === true → boolean handle x.inStock; ctx.x > 10 → gt(x,10)), with alias canonicalization matching eq.ts (ctx.req→ctx.request etc.). Anything with &&/||/typeof/!/.toLowerCase()/!=null/method calls/multi-field → leave as a raw when string (the legacy array branch DSL stays valid in hybrid) and emit a marker noting the new branch(handle) form cannot express it. Do NOT rewrite a $-proxy/$.-prefixed when into a handle blindly — that is the exact silent-undefined footgun; detect and flag it.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] Single pure-path comparison → boolean handle or gt/eq/lt with correct alias canonicalization
- [ ] Compound/method/typeof/null-check whens left as raw strings on the legacy branch form with a marker
- [ ] A $-prefixed when (the footgun) is detected and flagged, never silently converted
- [ ] No when is changed in a way that flips truthiness vs the original raw-ctx eval

**Edge cases to test:**
- ctx.request.method.toLowerCase() === "get" && ctx.request.params.function === undefined (real shipped case) → must stay raw + marked
- === true / === false collapse to boolean handle vs explicit eq(...,true)
- Comparison against undefined vs null vs '' — JSON.stringify(undefined) is invalid; must preserve original literal exactly

*estimate M · depends: HYBRID-RUNNER*

tasks.title: branch.when truthiness-equivalence test across the raw-ctx footgun corpus
tasks.body:
For each real shipped when (countries-vs-facts.json, the v05-* compound whens, dashboard-gen, etc.), evaluate the ORIGINAL raw-ctx string and the codemod OUTPUT against a battery of synthetic ctx objects and assert identical truthiness on every input — the only acceptable migration is one that never changes which arm runs. Assert compound whens are left raw+marked and $-prefixed whens are flagged.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] Truthiness parity asserted across ≥20 synthetic ctx inputs for every converted when
- [ ] Compound/method whens asserted left raw + marked
- [ ] $-prefixed-when footgun asserted flagged, not converted
- [ ] undefined/null/'' literal preservation asserted

**Edge cases to test:**
- Short-circuit: a && b where a is false and b would throw — both forms must agree
- Coercion edge: 0 / '' / NaN comparisons must match the original === semantics

*estimate M · depends: WHEN-CODEMOD*

tasks.title: Spike: does step() register via ambient builder side-effect or closure return? Pin the IR-production model
tasks.body:
The new branch(handle,{then:()=>..}) / forEach(handle,(item)=>..) closures require step() to record itself into the IR somehow. Resolve whether step() (a) mutates an ambient builder context (side-effect; breaks under conditional/async user code) or (b) the closure must RETURN its steps (pure; constrains authoring). Prototype both against 3 real control-flow workflows (v05-nested-control-flow.json, a forEach, a tryCatch) and determine which yields a deterministic IR equal to the array-form normalizer output. This decision blocks HYBRID-RUNNER's correctness and the safety of conditional step() calls. Output a recommendation + the failure modes of the rejected approach.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] Both models prototyped against ≥3 real nested-control-flow workflows
- [ ] Determinism + equivalence-to-array-IR assessed for each
- [ ] Failure modes of conditional/async step() inside closures documented
- [ ] A single recommended model with rationale, feeding HYBRID-RUNNER

**Edge cases to test:**
- if(cond) step(...) inside a branch arm closure
- step() called in a loop in user code (forEach body building N steps)
- async work between step() calls in a closure

*estimate M*

tasks.title: [gap] TEST: @blokjs/expr and non-HTTP (worker/pubsub/webhook) workflows survive migration byte-identically
tasks.body:
Two-part regression: (1) a workflow using @blokjs/expr passes through the field-aware codemod and the new DSL with its `expression` input UNCHANGED (no js/ prefix injected, no double-eval) and produces an identical trace; (2) worker, pubsub, and webhook workflows migrate their entry-surface references (ctx.request.body as job payload, params.{queue,jobId,attempt}, msg, event) correctly, including the legacy worker addTrigger() builder path.

**Edge cases:**
- expr expression containing the substring 'state.' must NOT be token-swapped
- worker ctx.vars._worker_job metadata access has no handle — must stay raw or be flagged
- pubsub msg vs http req — codemod must not assume req.body
- webhook event payload shape
- mixed-mode file (handle DSL + leftover js/) must be rejected by the schemaVersion gate

tasks.title: [gap] Rewrite the canonical authoring docs + AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md for the handle model
tasks.body:
Rewrite docs/d/fundamentals/context-and-state.mdx (the canonical context guide CLAUDE.md pins) and update AGENTS.md + CLAUDE.md to teach the handle/entry-handle/tpl/branch-op model and REMOVE the $/js/ctx authoring guidance, while keeping the node-side ctx ABI section (defineNode keeps ctx). Document the four control-flow footguns, the ephemeral-handle ban, and the escape hatch for non-structural expressions. Required by the standing 'keep these rules and that page in sync' rule.

**Edge cases:**
- the node-side ctx (publish/signal/connection/stream/logger) must remain documented as KEPT
- legacy v1->v2 mapping table needs a third column for the handle DSL
- worker legacy builder doc vs new form
- the branch-when raw-ctx footgun must be re-explained for boolean handles

epics.title: IR to canvas round-trip + separate layout.json - adversarial validation
epics.body:
Lossless round-trip is sound only under S4 Option A (JSON IR is the single source of truth; canvas re-derives the DAG every edit). The engine claim (PersistenceHelper/Mapper byte-identical) is untouched - round-trip is structural, not runtime. Three soundness gaps break against real code and the existing specs. (1) buildWorkflowDag does NOT key nodes by step id - it mints synthetic, build-order-dependent ids (step-1, branch-2, merge-3) via a monotonic idCounter and stashes the real id only in meta.stepId; synthetic nodes (merge/trigger/end/catchEnter/finallyEnter) have NO step id. A layout.json keyed by step id cannot key off the rendered node id and cannot store positions for synthetic nodes - the keying scheme is underspecified and partly impossible as stated. (2) The epic's { $ref: { step, path } } IR is net-new and contradicts S1/S4/S5, which all describe js/ctx.state.id strings compiled from the $ proxy at definition time; no $ref encoding exists in the codebase or specs. Round-trip, rename propagation, and the connect-picker must target whichever encoding actually ships. (3) The epic mandates a separate layout.json; S4 section 7.8 recommends an inline pass-through ui:{x,y} field - mutually exclusive designs that must be reconciled first. READ round-trip of branch/switch/forEach/tryCatch nesting is already lossless via buildWorkflowDag; every break lives in the WRITE path.

**Verdict:** `needs-rework`

**Risks / hard cases found in validation:**
- SYNTHETIC, BUILD-ORDER-DEPENDENT NODE IDS. buildWorkflowDag (apps/studio/src/lib/workflowDag.ts:165-167) generates node ids from a monotonic idCounter (step-N, branch-N, merge-N), NOT from step.id. Inserting or deleting any step renumbers every downstream synthetic id, so a layout.json keyed by these ids corrupts on every structural edit. The epic correctly wants to key by step id - but the renderer never exposes step id AS the node id (it lives in meta.stepId), so the write path must thread a stable stepId-to-position map through layoutDag, and that map cannot exist for the synthetic nodes below.
- SYNTHETIC NODES HAVE NO STEP ID, so a step-id-keyed layout.json structurally cannot position them. merge, trigger, end, catchEnter, finallyEnter are created with no meta.stepId (workflowDag.ts:312,344,432-438,453-454,546,556). tryCatch's try lane reuses the parent step id on tryEnter only; catch/finally enters are anonymous. If a user drags a merge diamond or the End pill, there is nowhere to store that position keyed by step id. Either these stay dagre-only (manual drags silently discarded) or the layout key needs a composite scheme. The epic's flat step-id keying does not cover this.
- THE $ref IR DOES NOT EXIST AND CONTRADICTS S1/S4/S5. The shipped and spec'd reference encoding is the string js/ctx.state.id (or $.state.id in JSON), produced by unwrapProxies at definition time (core/workflow-helper/src/proxy/$.ts:143-177) and resolved by the runtime Mapper. S1 never introduces a structural $ref node; S4/S5 treat references as opaque strings Studio must not parse. The epic's connect-picker-emits-valid-$ref and rename-breaks-$ref framing validates an encoding not in the codebase. If the proposed-core replaces the string form with $ref, that is a foundational IR change owned by S1 and must be specced/validated there FIRST.
- LAYOUT STORAGE CONFLICT: separate layout.json vs inline ui:{x,y}. S4 section 7.8 recommends positions as an optional pass-through ui:{x,y} on the step (runner-ignored, no format break). The epic mandates a separate layout.json keyed by step id. Both are defensible but mutually exclusive and imply different write paths, staleness semantics, and synthetic-node handling. Shipping one while a spec recommends the other guarantees rework.
- LAYOUT STALENESS / ORPHANING ON STRUCTURAL EDITS. A layout.json keyed by step id goes stale the instant a step is renamed (key no longer matches), deleted (orphan entry), or duplicated. renameStep must rewrite the layout key, deleteStep must purge it, and the loader must tolerate orphan/missing keys (fall back to dagre). None of this is in the epic. A missing key must NOT crash the canvas.
- STEP-ID RENAME PROPAGATION OVER- AND UNDER-MATCH. S4 section 7.5 proposes a word-boundary token swap across inputs strings. Adversarial cases: (a) over-match - a literal string value containing state.fetch gets silently rewritten; (b) under-match - references inside subworkflow polymorphic js/ expressions, inside branch.when/loop.while/switch.on (which use raw ctx.state.id WITHOUT the $ prefix - the known branch-when-raw-ctx footgun), and inside tpl backtick templates if the new authoring lands; (c) a step id that is a regex metacharacter or a substring of another id (fetch vs fetchUser). Rename cannot be proven safe without enumerating every reference site the runtime actually resolves.
- CONNECT-PICKER MUST EMIT PERSISTENCE-SHAPE-CORRECT REFERENCES, NOT state.id. A spread:true step merges result.data keys into the top of state (state.key); as:x stores under state.x; ephemeral:true stores nothing. A picker that builds ports from raw step ids emits references the runner cannot resolve. S5 section 7.4 flags this and recommends building ports from the hydrated ctx.state projection. The epic's picker inherits this hazard and does not mention spread/as/ephemeral.
- EPHEMERAL POSITIONS VS PERSISTED LAYOUT RACE. dagre re-layouts on every draft change (layoutDag, WorkflowGraph.tsx:133-167). If the user drags (persisting) then makes a structural edit (dagre re-runs), the dragged position must be honored as a seed AND pinned, or every structural edit silently undoes manual layout. layoutDag currently ignores seeds; seeding via setNode does NOT pin - dagre still moves it. Pinning requires a post-layout override for keyed nodes. Non-trivial and unspecified.
- TS-AUTHORED WORKFLOWS CANNOT ROUND-TRIP. S4 section 7.1 refuses canvas writes to TS workflows (round-trip drops Zod input/output schemas). The round-trip claim holds for JSON workflows only. layout.json for a TS workflow persists positions for a definition the canvas cannot otherwise edit - coherent only if layout is decoupled from definition writes. The epic should state round-trip is JSON-only and layout.json is allowed even for read-only TS workflows.
- DUPLICATE-ID IS A BLOCKING ERROR, NOT SOFT LINT. WorkflowNormalizer throws on duplicate step ids across all arms at load. Any write path (insertStep with a non-unique id, renameStep into a collision, connect that duplicates) that produces a duplicate yields a file the runner rejects on reload. The write path must reject duplicate ids as a hard error before save, and nextId(draft, kind) must guarantee global uniqueness across nested arms, not just the top level.

**Open questions to resolve:**
- Does the proposed-core actually replace js/ctx.state.id strings with structural $ref:{step,path} in the persisted IR, or is $ref only an in-memory authoring/handle representation that COMPILES DOWN to the existing string form (as the $ proxy does today)? This determines whether the canvas round-trip targets strings or $ref, and whether rename is a regex swap or a structural rewrite. Must be resolved in S1 before this epic is buildable.
- Separate layout.json or inline ui:{x,y}? If separate: where does it live (next to the workflow file? one studio-layouts.json? localStorage like S5 pin-data?), and is it committed or gitignored? Co-location and VCS semantics differ sharply.
- How are positions stored for synthetic nodes (merge, end, trigger, catchEnter, finallyEnter) with no step id? Options: (a) make them non-draggable/dagre-only; (b) composite key (ownerStepId + arm-role); (c) give every emitted node a stable derived id. Pick one before building the write path.
- Is connect-picker emit in scope for THIS epic or owned by S5? S5 section 7.4 owns the picker and the upstream-only port model. The epic lists picker emit as an attack surface but the logic lives in S5. Clarify the boundary so the picker test lands in the right epic.
- Should layoutDag pin keyed (manually-dragged) nodes after dagre runs, or seed-and-let-dagre-move them? Pinning is required for manual layout to survive structural edits; confirm that is the intended behavior (more work than the S4 seed-and-honor wording implies).

tasks.title: Spike: nail down the reference encoding ($ref vs js/ctx string) the canvas must round-trip
tasks.body:
Resolve the foundational ambiguity: does the persisted IR use structural $ref:{step,path} or the existing js/ctx.state.id string (compiled from the $ proxy, core/workflow-helper/src/proxy/$.ts)? Inspect S1-workflow-json-ir.md and current unwrapProxies output. Produce a one-page decision: the exact on-disk shape the canvas reads/writes, whether $ref is in-memory-only and compiles to strings, and the implications for rename propagation (regex token-swap vs structural rewrite) and connect-picker emit. Block all impl tasks below until answered.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] A written decision states the exact persisted reference shape with a concrete example for a regular step, a spread step, and an as-renamed step
- [ ] Decision states whether rename propagation operates on strings (regex) or structural nodes
- [ ] Decision is reconciled with S1 or files an S1 change request

**Edge cases to test:**
- spread/as/ephemeral steps produce a different reference shape than state.id
- branch.when/loop.while/switch.on use raw ctx.state.id WITHOUT the $ prefix
- subworkflow polymorphic expressions embed references inside js/ strings

*estimate M*

tasks.title: Spike: separate layout.json vs inline ui:{x,y}, and synthetic-node positioning
tasks.body:
Decide layout storage. Compare the epic's separate layout.json (keyed by step id) against S4 section 7.8's inline ui:{x,y} pass-through. Determine the key scheme that covers synthetic nodes (merge/trigger/end/catchEnter/finallyEnter in workflowDag.ts:312,344,432-438,453-454,546,556) which have no step id. Decide VCS/location semantics and staleness handling (orphan keys on delete/rename). Output a concrete schema for the layout artifact and the key derivation rule for every DagNodeKind.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] A concrete layout schema with a worked example keyed for a regular step, a branch (decision + merge), and a tryCatch (tryEnter + catchEnter + merge)
- [ ] A rule covers how synthetic nodes are positioned (dagre-only vs composite key)
- [ ] Staleness policy on rename/delete/duplicate is stated

**Edge cases to test:**
- a merge node sits between two arms and belongs to no single step
- tryCatch finally lane is conditionally emitted, shifting which nodes exist
- step ids are unique per-workflow not global, so a single global layout file keyed by step id alone collides across workflows

*estimate M*

tasks.title: Make DAG node ids stable and step-id-derived (replace build-order idCounter)
tasks.body:
Change buildWorkflowDag (apps/studio/src/lib/workflowDag.ts) so emitted node ids are deterministic and stable across structural edits - derive from step.id plus a synthetic-role suffix (e.g. stepId, stepId::merge, stepId::catch) instead of the monotonic idCounter (lines 165-167). Synthetic terminals (trigger/end) get fixed ids. Prerequisite for any step-id-keyed layout to survive insert/delete. Preserve all existing edge/label behavior; only id derivation changes.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] Inserting or deleting a step does NOT change the node id of any unrelated step
- [ ] Every node id is reconstructable from the step it belongs to plus a role suffix
- [ ] All existing workflowDag.test.ts assertions still pass or are updated with equivalent coverage

**Edge cases to test:**
- a step with no id still needs a deterministic node id
- two synthetic nodes of the same role under the same owner (nested tryCatch) must not collide
- empty arms (branch with no else, empty forEach body) must not produce dangling/duplicate ids

*estimate L · depends: SPIKE-LAYOUT-MODEL*

tasks.title: TEST: node-id stability across insert/delete/reorder and synthetic-node collisions
tasks.body:
Adversarial suite for IMPL-NODEID-STABILITY. Build a workflow, snapshot node ids, then insert a step at the front, delete a middle step, reorder, and assert unrelated node ids are byte-identical before/after. Add nested tryCatch-inside-branch-inside-forEach to force synthetic-role collisions and assert id uniqueness. Assert id-less steps still get stable ids.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] Test fails on the OLD idCounter scheme and passes on the new derivation
- [ ] Covers insert-at-front (worst case for build-order numbering), delete-middle, reorder
- [ ] Asserts global uniqueness of node ids in a 3-level-nested control-flow workflow

**Edge cases to test:**
- insert at index 0 renumbers everything under the old scheme
- two branches each with a merge
- tryCatch with and without finally in the same workflow

*estimate M · depends: IMPL-NODEID-STABILITY*

tasks.title: Structural edit ops on the JSON IR (findStepLocation + insert/delete/reorder/connect) with lossless arm nesting
tasks.body:
Implement editOps.ts per S4 section 7.4: findStepLocation(draft, id) (single arm-walker reused by all ops), insertStep, deleteStep, reorderStep, connect - operating on the real runner field names (do/then/else/try/catch/finally/cases[].do, confirmed in workflowDag.ts:314-456). All ops mutate a draft copy of the whole definition; buildWorkflowDag(draft) re-renders. nextId(draft, kind) must guarantee GLOBAL uniqueness across all nested arms (duplicate id = runner load-time throw). connect rejects cross-arm targets.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] Insert into branch.then, forEach.do, switch.cases[n].do, tryCatch.catch each land in the correct array and survive a buildWorkflowDag round-trip unchanged
- [ ] nextId never collides with an id in any nested arm
- [ ] deleteStep removes the step from whichever arm owns it and leaves no dangling array entry
- [ ] connect rejects a target in a different arm with a clear error

**Edge cases to test:**
- insert into an empty arm vs a populated arm
- delete the only step in an arm (arm becomes empty array, not undefined)
- reorder across the boundary of a nested control-flow step
- a step id equal to a JS reserved word or containing regex metacharacters

*estimate L · depends: IMPL-NODEID-STABILITY*

tasks.title: TEST: lossless round-trip of branch/switch/forEach/tryCatch nesting through edit ops
tasks.body:
Property-style test: a fixture with branch (then+else), switch (cases+default), forEach, loop, and tryCatch (try+catch+finally) nested 2-3 levels deep. Run buildWorkflowDag, then a sequence of insert/delete/reorder/connect ops, and assert the resulting JSON IR (a) still validates against WorkflowV2Schema, (b) preserves every untouched step byte-identically, (c) places edited steps in the correct arm. Include the empty-arm and no-else/no-default cases the renderer special-cases (workflowDag.ts:320-326,355-367).

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] A deeply nested fixture survives 10 random edit ops with no untouched step mutated
- [ ] Result validates against the published WorkflowV2 schema after every op
- [ ] Empty-else / empty-default / empty-forEach-body cases round-trip without spurious arms

**Edge cases to test:**
- branch with no else arm (dashed direct-to-merge edge in renderer)
- switch with no default
- tryCatch with no finally vs with finally (changes exit node)
- forEach with empty do (header only, no back-edge)

*estimate M · depends: IMPL-ROUNDTRIP-WRITE*

tasks.title: renameStep with reference propagation across every documented reference site
tasks.body:
Implement renameStep(draft, oldId, newId) per the encoding decided in SPIKE-IR-ENCODING. If strings: rewrite the token across inputs strings for BOTH $. and js/ctx. prefixes AND the vars alias AND raw-ctx forms in branch.when/loop.while/switch.on (which use ctx.state.id without $), AND subworkflow polymorphic expressions. If $ref structural: rewrite $ref.step fields. Reject newId collisions (reuse the global id-set). Also rewrite the layout key for the renamed node. Return a rewrite count for the author to verify (over-match surfacing).

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] Renaming a step updates references in inputs, branch.when/loop.while/switch.on, and subworkflow expressions
- [ ] newId collision is rejected before any mutation
- [ ] the layout.json key for the node follows the rename
- [ ] a rewrite count is returned

**Edge cases to test:**
- oldId is a substring of another id (fetch vs fetchUser) - must not partial-match
- oldId appears inside a literal string value, not a reference (over-match) - surfaced via count
- reference inside a raw-ctx when expression without the $ prefix
- id containing regex metacharacters

*estimate L · depends: SPIKE-IR-ENCODING, IMPL-ROUNDTRIP-WRITE*

tasks.title: TEST: rename propagation over/under-match and layout-key follow-through
tasks.body:
Adversarial rename tests. (1) Under-match guard: a reference in branch.when (raw ctx.state.id), in a subworkflow polymorphic expression, and in a tpl/template string must all be rewritten. (2) Over-match surfacing: a literal input value containing state.id as data is rewritten AND the count reflects it. (3) Substring safety: renaming fetch must not touch fetchUser. (4) Collision: rename into an existing id is rejected. (5) Layout: the position keyed to oldId is re-keyed to newId, not orphaned.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] Every documented reference site is covered by an assertion
- [ ] Substring (fetch/fetchUser) case proves word-boundary correctness
- [ ] Collision rename throws and leaves the draft unmutated
- [ ] Layout key follows the rename

**Edge cases to test:**
- raw-ctx when without $ prefix
- subworkflow js/ expression reference
- literal data string false-positive (count > real refs)
- metacharacter id

*estimate M · depends: IMPL-RENAME-PROP*

tasks.title: Layout persistence + dagre seed/pin and orphan tolerance
tasks.body:
Implement the layout artifact decided in SPIKE-LAYOUT-MODEL: write manual drag positions keyed per the decided scheme; on load, seed layoutDag (WorkflowGraph.tsx:133-167) with stored positions and PIN keyed nodes after dagre runs so structural edits do not overwrite manual layout. Tolerate orphan keys (deleted steps) and missing keys (fall back to dagre) without crashing. Discard drag deltas for nodes with no layout key (synthetic, per the spike decision) or store them under the composite key.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] A manual drag persists and survives a subsequent structural edit (dagre re-layout) instead of snapping back
- [ ] An orphan layout key (step deleted) is ignored and pruned, no crash
- [ ] A missing key falls back to dagre auto-layout
- [ ] Synthetic nodes are handled per the spike decision (dagre-only or composite key)

**Edge cases to test:**
- drag a node, then insert an upstream step (dagre re-runs) - dragged node stays put
- delete a dragged step - its layout entry is purged
- open a workflow whose layout.json predates a rename - stale key ignored gracefully
- TS-authored (read-only) workflow still allows layout-only persistence

*estimate L · depends: IMPL-NODEID-STABILITY, SPIKE-LAYOUT-MODEL*

tasks.title: TEST: ephemeral position vs persisted layout race + orphan/missing-key resilience
tasks.body:
The epic's named attack surface. (1) Drag node A (persist), insert upstream step B (force dagre re-layout), assert A's position is unchanged (pinned, not snapped back). (2) Delete a dragged step, assert its layout entry is pruned and no stale key remains. (3) Load a layout.json with an orphan key and a missing key, assert the canvas renders (orphan ignored, missing falls back to dagre). (4) Assert dragging a synthetic merge node behaves per the spike decision.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] Pinned node survives a dagre re-layout triggered by a structural edit
- [ ] Orphan key is pruned; missing key falls back to dagre; neither crashes
- [ ] Synthetic-node drag behavior matches the spike decision
- [ ] A rename re-keys the layout entry (cross-check with TEST-RENAME-PROP)

**Edge cases to test:**
- dagre seed without pin would move the node - test proves pin, not just seed
- layout file with keys for steps that no longer exist
- empty layout file
- layout for a TS read-only workflow

*estimate M · depends: IMPL-LAYOUT-WRITE*

tasks.title: TEST: write path rejects duplicate ids as a hard error before save
tasks.body:
The runner throws on duplicate step ids across all arms at load (WorkflowNormalizer). Prove the write path never produces a savable file the runner would reject: (1) insertStep into a branch arm cannot mint an id already used in the sibling arm or top level; (2) renameStep into any existing id is rejected; (3) a hand-crafted draft with a cross-arm duplicate id is caught by the pre-save validate/lint and surfaced as a blocking error, not a warning. Mirror the runner's cross-arm uniqueness check.

**Acceptance:**
- [ ] nextId is proven unique against a draft that already has ids in nested arms
- [ ] rename-into-collision is blocked
- [ ] cross-arm duplicate is a blocking error in the pre-save gate, matching the runner's throw semantics

**Edge cases to test:**
- duplicate id across mutually-exclusive branch then/else arms (runner still rejects)
- duplicate between top-level and a nested forEach.do step
- id reused via as: alias is allowed (not an id collision)

*estimate S · depends: IMPL-ROUNDTRIP-WRITE*

epics.title: Typed step() handles + $ref engine
epics.body:
Verdict: SOUND-WITH-RISKS. The surface (typed step() handles, tpl, branch-on-handle) is achievable in TS and is a real ergonomic win, but the headline "same {$ref} engine, byte-identical runtime" claim is FALSE as written and is the load-bearing risk the founder must not ship on faith.

GROUNDED FINDINGS (verified against real code):

1. {$ref} IS NOT RESOLVED BY TODAY'S MAPPER — CONFIRMED. core/shared/src/utils/Mapper.ts:269-288 `replaceObjectStrings` only transforms values where `typeof value === "string"` (via replaceString→jsMapper, which itself no-ops unless the string starts with "js/", Mapper.ts:361). For object values it recurses ONLY into plain containers (isPlainContainer, Mapper.ts:235). A structural `{ $ref: { step, path } }` is a plain object → the mapper recurses INTO it, finds `step`/`path` (strings without a js/ prefix → passed through unchanged), and the `{$ref}` wrapper survives intact. The node's execute() receives the literal `{$ref:{...}}` object, then Zod input validation rejects it. So the redesign CANNOT reuse today's engine untouched. Exactly one of two things must happen and the deck must pick one explicitly: (A) the DSL lowers every handle read to a `"js/ctx.state.<step>.<path>"` STRING at definition time (reuses the mapper byte-for-byte, but then `{$ref}` is a documentation fiction — the IR is still js/ strings, and the canvas/JSON-twin story in the deck that shows `{$ref}` in the wire JSON is wrong); or (B) the mapper gains a real `{$ref}` branch (a genuine engine change — "byte-identical" is false, and it needs its own resolution + error-context + idempotency-cache-key audit). The deck currently asserts BOTH "same engine" AND "{$ref} in the IR" — they are mutually exclusive. This is the #1 thing to nail down.

2. branch-on-handle has a SECOND, worse lowering problem. branch.when is NOT mapper-resolved at all: the if-else node (nodes/control-flow/if-else@1.0.0/index.ts:73-74) evals `condition.condition` as a RAW string via `Function("ctx",...)`. So a boolean handle passed to branch() cannot lower to a `{$ref}` (the if-else node takes a string, not an object) and cannot lower to a `js/` string either (the evaluator strips no prefix — `js/ctx...` evals as a division expression and throws or mis-evaluates, the exact footgun documented in eq.ts:5-12 and memory blok-branch-when-raw-ctx). A handle in branch MUST lower to a bare `ctx.state.<step>.<path>` raw-ctx string — a THIRD lowering distinct from inputs ({$ref} or js/) and from tpl. So the clean "one {$ref} surface" story actually compiles to three different wire forms depending on position (input value / inside tpl / branch condition). That is fine engineering but it directly contradicts the deck's "identical resolution engine, one surface" claim and must be designed deliberately, not discovered at runtime.

3. tpl avoids the toString-coercion trap ONLY IF it captures interpolations as structured segments BEFORE coercion. The existing $ proxy already fights this (Symbol.toPrimitive/toString return `js/${prefix}`, $.ts:93-96) — a tagged template literal `tpl\`...${handle.field}...\`` receives the RAW handle object in its `values` array (no coercion yet), so tpl CAN capture the {$ref}/path structurally. The trap is real but avoidable: the risk is any code path that lets a handle hit string concatenation or `${}` interpolation OUTSIDE tpl (e.g. `\`x${handle}\`` without the tag) — there it silently coerces via toString to whatever string the handle's toString emits, with no compile error. Needs a toString that throws (or returns a poison marker) so misuse is loud.

4. The typed Proxy is SOUND for the common cases (nested object fields, array index, optional chaining at the type level) but has known TS holes the deck doesn't acknowledge: (a) union output types — `T = {a:number}|{b:string}` — a handle proxy typed as the union lets you read `.a` only with narrowing the proxy can't carry; (b) array element access `handle.items[0].sku` needs the proxy's index type to recurse, and `.map()/.length/.find()` are real Array members the type will expose but the runtime proxy can't honor (reading `.length` records a {$ref:{path:["length"]}} that resolves to the array length at runtime — sometimes correct, usually a footgun); (c) a whole-output ref (`stock` passed as one input) vs a field ref must be distinguishable in the recorded {$ref} (path:[] vs path:[...]) — the deck shows both but never says how the proxy tags "this is the leaf, stop here." These are designable but each is a TEST surface.

5. Handle identity across branch arms / closures is UNVERIFIED and is where auto-persistence meets lexical scope. The deck's killer example is `stock` declared outside a branch and read inside `then: () => {...}`. That works at the VALUE level (stock is a closure variable holding a {$ref} to step "checkStock"). But: (a) step() has a SIDE EFFECT (it must register the step into the workflow's step list in execution order) AND returns a handle — calling step() inside a `then` callback must append to the THEN arm's step list, not the top level. That means step() needs ambient "current arm" state (a module-level builder stack) that the callback-style factory pushes/pops around each arm. The deck shows `workflow(id, opts, (req)=>...)` and `branch(cond, {then: ()=>..., else: ()=>...})` but never shows the registration mechanism. If step() uses a global "current builder" and an author calls step() outside any workflow() callback, or stores a step thunk and runs it later, registration goes to the wrong place or throws. This is the single biggest implementation risk and the deck is silent on it.

6. A handle read when its step NEVER RAN (skipped branch arm, ephemeral, or errored) resolves against `state[step] === undefined`. Today that's a truthful undefined (Rule 0, PersistenceHelper.ts). With handles the author SEES a typed non-optional field and reasonably assumes it's present — the type system now LIES (the field is typed `string` but is `undefined` at runtime because the producing step was in the untaken arm). This is a regression in honesty vs the string form, where `$.state.x` at least visually signals "late-bound." Needs a design answer (handles from a branch arm should be typed optional outside that arm; or reading a cross-arm handle is a compile error).

7. Perf: tpl and {$ref} resolution must NOT recompile a Function per forEach iteration. Today jsMapper calls `Function(...)` on every replaceString (Mapper.ts:418) — already a per-step cost, and inside forEach that's per-item. The {$ref} path, if implemented as structural resolution (lodash _.get against state[step]), AVOIDS Function entirely and is strictly faster — a point in favor of lowering (B). But if the DSL lowers to js/ strings (A), forEach inner steps recompile the same Function N times. An LRU cache keyed on the expression string fixes it and is a cheap, separable win. The deck claims "identical runtime" — it should claim "faster for {$ref}, needs an LRU for the js/-string fallback."

OPEN QUESTIONS the founder must answer before build: (Q1) lowering A (js/ strings, reuse engine, {$ref} is fiction) vs B (real {$ref} mapper branch, engine changes) — pick one, the whole deck narrative depends on it.
